translation: Alif, Lam, Ra. [This is] a Book whose verses are perfected and then presented in detail from [one who is] Wise and Acquainted
commentary:
الٓر ۚ كِتَـٰبٌ أُحْكِمَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ

The surah begins with the disconnected letters Alif-Lam-Ra, connecting it thematically to surahs with similar openings. The word كِتَابٌ - "a book" - immediately follows, describing the Quran whose ayahs have been أُحْكِمَتْ - made profound and firm.

The term أُحْكِمَتْ carries multiple layers of meaning. It indicates that the ayahs are filled with wisdom and divine instruction. It also means they have been kept brief and comprehensive - few words capturing immense wisdom. This is the beauty of Quranic expression: concise yet deep, accessible yet profound.

Then comes ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ - "then they were explained in detail." The word فُصِّلَتْ comes from the root meaning to separate, to distinguish, to explain thoroughly. Just as a teacher separates each concept to explain it carefully, Allah ﷻ takes what He has made concise and expounds upon it. This is exactly what happens between Surah Yunus and Surah Hud: Yunus mentions the destruction of nations briefly, while Hud explains these accounts in detail.

This divine explanation comes مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ - "from One who is All-Wise, All-Aware." The source of this book is Allah ﷻ, who possesses perfect wisdom (Hakim) and complete knowledge (Khabir) of all affairs.

This opening ayah establishes a crucial pedagogical principle. Even a child can memorize Surah Al-Ikhlas or Al-Kawthar - brief, simple surahs. But as that child grows, their understanding deepens. The same verses they memorized as children become richer in meaning as they mature. This is the methodology Allah ﷻ describes here: begin with what is easy to grasp and remember, then layer understanding upon understanding over time.

translation: [Through a messenger, saying], "Do not worship except Allah. Indeed, I am to you from Him a warner and a bringer of good tidings
commentary:
أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّنِى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ

After establishing the methodology, Allah ﷻ presents the objective: أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ  - "that you not worship except Allah." This is the essence, the goal, the purpose of all this divine communication. The entire structure of brief revelation followed by detailed explanation exists for one reason: to guide humanity to worship Allah ﷻ alone.

The Prophet ﷺ then declares his role: إِنَّنِى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ - "Indeed, I am to you from Him a warner and a bringer of good news." Notice the order: نَذِير (warner) comes before بَشِير (bearer of good news). This sequencing tells us something significant about the tone of this surah. There will be more warning than glad tidings here, which explains why the accounts of destroyed nations are so prominent.

translation: And [saying], "Seek forgiveness of your Lord and repent to Him, [and] He will let you enjoy a good provision for a specified term and give every doer of favor his favor. But if you turn away, then indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a great Day
commentary:
وَأَنِ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يُمَتِّعْكُم مَّتَـٰعًا حَسَنًا إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى وَيُؤْتِ كُلَّ ذِى فَضْلٍ فَضْلَهُۥ ۖ وَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ كَبِيرٍ

Having established the goal of worshiping Allah ﷻ alone, this ayah presents the practical path: وَأَنِ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ - "And seek forgiveness of your Lord and then repent to Him."

Notice the crucial distinction Allah ﷻ makes here between إِسْتِغْفَار and تَوْبَة. While we often use these terms interchangeably, Allah ﷻ separates them with the word ثُمَّ ("then"), indicating a sequence. Istighfar is the first step - acknowledging your mistake, apologizing to Allah ﷻ, admitting what you've done wrong. It involves humility and embarrassment before your Lord. Then comes tawbah, which is the second step: actively returning to Allah ﷻ and taking practical measures not to fall into that sin again. It's the transition toward the light, the turning of your entire being back to Him.

The reward for this two-step process is immediate and worldly before being eternal: يُمَتِّعْكُم مَّتَـٰعًا حَسَنًا إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى - "He will grant you good enjoyment until a specified term." Allah ﷻ promises that simply through seeking forgiveness and repenting, you will have a good life in this world, until the appointed time that He has determined. The phrase أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى means a named, specified, well-described time - a time known fully to Allah ﷻ with all its details.

Then comes another beautiful promise: وَيُؤْتِ كُلَّ ذِى فَضْلٍ فَضْلَهُۥ- "And He will give every possessor of favor his favor." This means Allah ﷻ will appreciate and reward every good deed. Anyone who does good will receive what they deserve of Allah's favor and blessing. No good deed goes unnoticed or unrewarded by Him.

But then the tone shifts: وَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ كَبِيرٍ - "But if you turn away, then indeed I fear for you the punishment of a great Day." The verb تَوَلَّوْا۟ can be understood in two ways grammatically: "if you [plural] turn away" or "if they turn away." Given the context of second-person address throughout these ayahs (يُمَتِّعْكُم - "He will grant you"), it appears to be addressing the listeners directly: if you turn away from this message of seeking forgiveness and repentance, then the Prophet ﷺ fears for you the punishment of a tremendous Day.

translation: To Allah is your return, and He is over all things competent
commentary:
إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

This brief but powerful ayah establishes two fundamental realities: إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ - "To Allah is your return" - and وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ - "And He is over all things competent."

The first statement reminds us that our ultimate destination is back to Allah ﷻ. No matter what we do in this life, where we go, what we achieve, we are all returning to Him. The second statement establishes His absolute power and control. He has complete authority over all affairs, including our return to Him and what follows.

translation: Unquestionably, they the disbelievers turn away their breasts to hide themselves from Him. Unquestionably, [even] when they cover themselves in their clothing, Allah knows what they conceal and what they declare. Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts
commentary:
أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ لِيَسْتَخْفُوا۟ مِنْهُ ۚ أَلَا حِينَ يَسْتَغْشُونَ ثِيَابَهُمْ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ

This ayah addresses a specific psychology - those who hide the truth they know inside their hearts. The phrase يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ literally means "they fold up their chests" or "they cover up their chests." The verb comes from the concept of covering something, concealing it.

This has been interpreted in various ways by scholars. Some understood it literally, referring to people who were extremely modest, even covering themselves completely while bathing. However, the contextual interpretation is more fitting here: these are people who fold up their motives, who cover up the secret of iman (faith) inside their chests. Deep down, they recognize the truth of the message. They know they have no real response to Islam. They're impressed by it internally. But their pride, their stubbornness, their attachment to their former way of life prevents them from acknowledging it.

The reason for this concealment: لِيَسْتَخْفُوا۟ مِنْهُ - "so they may hide from Him." They want to keep this internal recognition hidden from Allah ﷻ, as if that were possible. They don't want anyone, not even Allah ﷻ, to know that deep inside they're moved by this message.

How do they manifest this externally? When the Prophet ﷺ would pass by, some would grab their clothes dramatically, pull them closer, and say, "Get away from us, you're filthy!" They would act as though they were pure and the Muslims were contaminated, creating a show of superiority and disgust. This condescending attitude, this looking down upon others, becomes a defense mechanism to hide what they truly feel inside.

Allah ﷻ then exposes the futility of this concealment: أَلَا حِينَ يَسْتَغْشُونَ ثِيَابَهُمْ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ - "Unquestionably, even when they cover themselves with their garments, He knows what they conceal and what they declare." You can pull your clothes over yourself all you want, you can create whatever external show you wish, but Allah ﷻ already knows what you're hiding inside and what you're displaying outside.

The ayah concludes with a powerful statement: إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ - "Indeed, He is All-Knowing of the nature of the breasts." The word ذَات here refers to the very essence, the nature, the reality of what lies within the chest. Allah ﷻ doesn't just know the surface thoughts; He knows the deepest recesses of the heart, the hidden motives, the secrets people barely acknowledge to themselves.

This theme of arrogance and condescension, of thinking oneself superior while knowing the truth internally, will recur throughout Surah Hud. As we encounter the stories of past nations, we'll see this same attitude manifest again and again - people who look down upon the messengers and their followers, who use insults and mockery to hide their internal recognition of the truth. Allah ﷻ is preparing us to understand this psychological pattern before we witness it in action through the accounts that follow.

translation: And there is no creature on earth but that upon Allah is its provision, and He knows its place of dwelling and place of storage. All is in a clear register
commentary:
۞ وَمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ رِزْقُهَا وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّهَا وَمُسْتَوْدَعَهَا ۚ كُلٌّ فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ

There is not a single beast that walks on the Earth except that Allah ﷻ Himself has taken account of its provisions. Allah ﷻ Himself is responsible for providing for it. This ayah delivers a different kind of warning to those who are arrogant about revelation. If you think you have time, if you haven't seen any punishment yet, understand that your punishment could start immediately if Allah ﷻ were to take away your sustenance. You don't have to wait for Judgment Day—if your food and drink stop, if you can no longer take air into your lungs, you're finished. What creature walks on this earth that He is not providing for?

The phrase مُسْتَقَرَّهَا وَمُسْتَوْدَعَهَا carries multiple interpretations. Mustaqarr refers to the place where something stays for a long time, while mustawda' is a place where something is kept temporarily for storage before being taken out. Allah ﷻ is saying He knows the place where you will remain (where you walk around on earth) and your final storage place (your grave from which you will eventually be brought out). All of this is recorded in كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ —an open book, a clear book that clarifies everything.

The grave itself carries profound symbolism in Arabic imagery. Graves are a form of secret—they hide what's buried within them. Eventually, plants grow over them and you don't even know a graveyard was ever there. They disappear completely. The entire imagery is of something hidden inside. Yet Allah ﷻ says that even things hidden in the earth—He knows how long they will stay hidden and when they will be brought out, just like the secrets you carry inside yourself. Nothing is hidden from Allah ﷻ. All of it is in an open book, a clear book.

translation: And it is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days - and His Throne had been upon water - that He might test you as to which of you is best in deed. But if you say, "Indeed, you are resurrected after death," those who disbelieve will surely say, "This is not but obvious magic
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَكَانَ عَرْشُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلْمَآءِ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا ۗ وَلَئِن قُلْتَ إِنَّكُم مَّبْعُوثُونَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْمَوْتِ لَيَقُولَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ

He is the One who created the skies and the earth in six days, and His Throne was upon water. Some scholars derive from this that when Allah ﷻ created the earth, it was entirely covered in water. The Throne itself was positioned upon water, suggesting that water was the starting point of earthly creation itself.

Scholar Imam Al-Sharawi made an interesting observation about what connects Surah Yunus and Surah Hud thematically. His conclusion was water. Consider the connection between Prophets Nuh and Musa (AS): rain water, parting water. In this surah, we find the Throne upon water. By the end of the surah, we'll read about which messenger? Prophet Nuh (AS)—and what do you think of when you think of Nuh (AS)? Water. The flood. Before that, we encounter Prophet Salih (AS), and when you think of Salih (AS), you think of the she-camel and water.

لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا—He did all of this so He may test which one of you are best in deeds. The earth and the heavens were created the way they were, all of it just for you to be placed in a test. When an examiner prepares an exam, they don't need much time for a simple test. But if you want to create an elaborate test, you must set up an elaborate examination environment. Sometimes psychological tests require subjects to be placed inside specially designed rooms with specific colors, sounds or visual stimuli. Social psychology experiments might involve setting up cameras in public spaces to observe human behavior.

The more elaborate your test, the more preparations required. Allah ﷻ created something so magnificent for testing human beings that He created the entire universe—the skies and the earth—all of it to test you. Consider the profound honor Allah ﷻ has given the human being. We are insignificant in size compared to the universe. If you've ever watched a documentary showing the vastness of space, those 3D renderings that zoom deeper and deeper into stars and galaxies, you feel lesser than an ant. You feel completely insignificant.

Modern science tells us we are just a speck in the universe, completely insignificant, with no worth or value. The logical conclusion they draw: since we're so small and insignificant, nobody will care what we do. We'll go unnoticed. But Allah ﷻ says: yes, you are insignificant in comparison to the creation, but Allah ﷻ has made all of that massive creation to test you. The focus is on you. The grandeur of the universe doesn't take attention away from you—it actually brings attention to you.

This is the exact opposite of modern scientific philosophy. The entire rationale of someone who rejects God's existence is: "The universe is way too big for God to worry about me. We're just on this rock floating in empty space. Eventually some meteor will hit and it'll all be over. Might as well party until it ends." You only live once—YOLO. But understand that these ideas don't originate on social media. They emanate from philosophers and intellectuals in books most people will never read. These abstract concepts eventually trickle down into society, permeating through culture until they become catchphrases.

The Quran presents the exact opposite worldview. وَلَئِن قُلْتَ إِنَّكُم مَّبْعُوثُونَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْمَوْتِ—If you were to say that you are all going to be raised after death, لَيَقُولَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ—then no doubt about it, those who disbelieve will swear: "This is nothing short of obvious magic. This is clearly magic. It can't be anything else."

translation: And if We hold back from them the punishment for a limited time, they will surely say, "What detains it?" Unquestionably, on the Day it comes to them, it will not be averted from them, and they will be enveloped by what they used to ridicule
commentary:
وَلَئِنْ أَخَّرْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ مَّعْدُودَةٍ لَّيَقُولُنَّ مَا يَحْبِسُهُۥٓ ۗ أَلَا يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمْ لَيْسَ مَصْرُوفًا عَنْهُمْ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

Even if We were to delay the punishment from them, if We were to give them a little more extra time—the word أُمَّة appears several times in the Quran meaning a time span. Along with ajal and waqt and muddah, ummah also carries this meaning. But ummah has a more common meaning: nation. The root letters are tied together with umm, which means mother. A woman who is expecting, hamil, carries within her a visible reminder of a limited time span, because within a certain period she will deliver. From this, the word came to mean a matter that's already bound to occur, already set to occur. The timer is already running. She's becoming a mother—the only thing left is delivery. The word thus carries the meaning of a time span at the end of which something is bound to happen.

إِلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ مَّعْدُودَةٍ—until a limited, numbered extension. Allah ﷻ specifically chose ummah rather than ajal or waqt because you cannot avoid what's coming. Even if I extended the time—not that I will, but even if I did—it's inevitable. It won't be put off forever. It's still an ummah.

مَا يَحْبِسُهُۥٓ—"What's holding it back?" they would say. Habs in Arabic literally means to hold someone down, like in a wrestling match when one fighter pins the other and won't let them get up, or in an arrest situation when someone is forced to sit on their knees and any attempt to rise is pushed back down. Mahbus is a person imprisoned in a tight cell, like a cage, where they cannot even stand up. When the punishment is promised and then extended, they mock: "What's holding the punishment back? Who beat up this punishment that it can't come out? I thought this punishment was supposed to be powerful. Who overpowered it? I want to see them."

Allah's response is devastating. أَلَا—listen up! This is one of those powerful particles in Arabic that functions as an entire sentence in itself. يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمْ—the day it comes at them, the day it attacks them, لَيْسَ مَصْرُوفًا عَنْهُمْ—it will not be turned away from them.

وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ—everything they used to mock will descend upon them and surround them. Istihza' is to make fun of something that hasn't happened yet. Haqa means to descend upon something and surround it completely—like a net falling on an animal and surrounding it, or floodwater rising around you with no escape.

Notice the contrast in imagery. The disbelievers imagine the punishment trying to get up but being held down—implying it's weak because it cannot rise. But when Allah ﷻ describes it, He doesn't describe it as something that will rise. Rather, it will fall upon them from above, come down on them like an axe. There's a powerful reversal: they think the punishment is weak and restrained, but Allah ﷻ reveals it as overwhelmingly powerful, descending from above.

translation: And if We give man a taste of mercy from Us and then We withdraw it from him, indeed, he is despairing and ungrateful
commentary:
وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً ثُمَّ نَزَعْنَـٰهَا مِنْهُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيَـُٔوسٌ كَفُورٌ

This ayah is absolutely central to understanding one of the greatest trials of our time: people losing their faith. Why do people stop believing? I've received calls from around the world—from Singapore, from various cities—young Muslims saying, "I don't believe anymore. I don't see the point." I've met countless youth in masajid whose mothers sent them to talk to me: "I'm not sure I believe in God anymore."

This ayah addresses the root of why a person leaves belief in Allah ﷻ. Earlier, I mentioned that later in this surah, Allah ﷻ will describe the mentality of someone who accepts Him versus someone who rejects Him, someone who accepts revelation versus someone who rejects it. These ayahs are preparing us for that discussion. If you understand this ayah, many secrets to these problems of faith are solved.

وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً—Even if and when We were to make the human being taste a mercy that especially comes from Us. Notice the construction: rahmatan minna. The word minna is attached to rahmah, forcing you to think of them together—a mercy that comes specifically from Us.

ثُمَّ نَزَعْنَـٰهَا مِنْهُ—then We pull it away from him. Naza'a in Arabic means to pull something that was well-rested, stationed in its place, not moving—and then you move it. Like when you move a piece of furniture that leaves a mark on the floor, or pull a bed that leaves an impression. It was comfortable there, set in place, and it's painful to move because it was so settled.

Allah ﷻ gives people luxuries and blessings, and they become so accustomed to them that they no longer think of them as luxuries. They think these things are just part of life, things they deserve. Then when Allah ﷻ pulls away something they were completely used to—إِنَّهُۥ لَيَـُٔوسٌ كَفُورٌ—no doubt about it, he doesn't go through a philosophical problem. He goes through an emotional problem.

لَيَـُٔوسٌ—the form is fa'ul, indicating intensity. He becomes extremely despairing, extremely hopeless: "There is no good in my life." When I talk to these youth who say they don't believe in God anymore, I don't ask what philosophy class they took or what book they read, because I know that's not the real reason. All that intellectual justification came later. I ask: "What happened?" Because something bad happened. "Did it have to do with your friends? Your parents? School?" One of those things. "When you're ready to talk about it, we can talk about God—because something happened."

Something you were used to, some mercy, some joy that Allah ﷻ had given you—it got taken away. And you went into serious depression. That depression evolved into kufr. What's the next word after ya'us? كَفُورٌ—excessively ungrateful. Once he became ya'us (despairing), the only thing left for him was kafur.

This pulling away doesn't have to be a physical thing. Let me give you an example that might seem trivial but illustrates the principle powerfully. For young girls, there's an intense need to belong—to belong to a group of friends, to be in a certain circle. One of the worst sadnesses a young girl experiences is being pulled out of her friend group. Three out of four girls might come and say, "You're not in our club anymore." She goes into serious depression. She feels like her whole world has fallen apart. She doesn't want to eat, study, play—nothing. Just sitting there crying.

I witnessed this with one of my daughters. Her friends went to town and bought friendship bracelets—three for 99 cents. But there were four friends, so they only bought three. The fourth one, because she didn't have a bracelet from the 99-cent store, could no longer be in the club. "You don't have one, so you can't be in the club. Therefore, you're not our friend anymore." The girl cried her heart out. They literally stopped talking to her because of no membership. It took over her life.

Now, this seems trivial and childish. But as girls get older, the dynamics intensify. In public schools—even Islamic schools aren't immune—there are cliques. Three girls in a group have boyfriends, and that's what makes them belong. The fourth one knows it's haram. "How come you don't have one? You're kind of weird." Now it's not that she wants a boyfriend—it's that she doesn't belong anymore. This sadness sinks in. She comes home and is told, "Stay away from those girls, they're not good for you." She gets even more depressed: "I can't even have friends. My parents are taking away my friendship." And in her mind: "Why are my parents taking away my friendship? It's Islam. It's God doing this to me."

What started as something seemingly innocent among four girls—three of whom have inadequate parenting—becomes "I want to leave Islam." All of it, because something was pulled away. Just a little sense of belonging was pulled away.

These things we think are trivial cannot be addressed by simply saying "Dating is haram in Islam" or listing prohibitions. That's not the real problem. You have to find the root problem before you can address it. This ayah addresses the root problem: there's something you really cherish in life, and it's pulled away from you. The first thing that happens is you go into depression.

The solution is found in our book, the Quran, and as we'll learn in this surah: In good times, you're grateful to Allah ﷻ. In tough times, you remain patient. But how do you find patience?

Consider this: is there any greater mercy than a child who brings you joy? A child in whom you see the potential for greatness, even in their character? There are difficult children who yell, scream, don't listen, fight with siblings—they're tests for their parents. But then there's a child who prays on his own, who wants to memorize Quran, who does his homework without being told, who cleans his own room, makes his own bed. And on top of that, he's pleasant to be around. That's like Prophet Yusuf (AS)—what a source of joy. You cannot experience a greater joy than that.

But what's going to happen in the next surah? That very joy is taken away from the father, Ya'qub (AS). What a devastating thing to experience. Yet when Ya'qub (AS) describes his situation, what word does he use? He says بَنِي (baniyya)—my son, my beautiful one. You have to focus on what's still beautiful in life. You have to find beauty in the ugliest situation. That's a believer.

When you focus on what you've lost, the depression will destroy you—لَيَـُٔوسٌ. And the worst destruction is to become كَفُورٌ—not just kafir, but kafur: excessively, intensely ungrateful and disbelieving.

translation: But if We give him a taste of favor after hardship has touched him, he will surely say, "Bad times have left me." Indeed, he is exultant and boastful
commentary:
وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَـٰهُ نَعْمَآءَ بَعْدَ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ ذَهَبَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتُ عَنِّىٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَفَرِحٌ فَخُورٌ

When we make the human being taste an extended favor—نَعْمَآءَ—after prolonged hardship that had touched him, his response reveals a deeper spiritual problem. Allah ﷻ describes how He opens not just a door of relief, but the floodgates of blessing. Consider the example of Prophet Yusuf (AS): Allah ﷻ didn't merely bring him out of prison, He brought him out as a ruler with authority.

The word مَسَّتْهُ means "it touched him," but the connotation is deeper—it poked at him, pinched him continuously. The hardship wasn't just present; it kept getting at him, persistently troubling him. Think of someone who has lost their job and spent months searching for employment. The first few weeks, they're actively networking and applying. When that doesn't yield results, they start showing up at the masjid more frequently, their prayers become more earnest, their supplications extend longer. But as the months drag on—six, seven, eight months—depression sets in. A person begins to feel worthless, questioning their very value.

This is one of the most devastating trials a person can face, particularly for men who often tie their sense of worth to their ability to provide. Then finally, after this prolonged period of darkness, someone calls with an interview opportunity. The interview goes well. Those eight, nine, ten months of depression suddenly lift.

But notice what Allah ﷻ says happens next: لَيَقُولَنَّ ذَهَبَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتُ عَنِّىٓ—"He will definitely say: My problems have disappeared from me!" The person celebrates their success as if they accomplished it themselves. Even if they say "Alhamdulillah," they often don't truly mean it. We've become accustomed to saying these phrases without connecting them to their reality. The person becomes فَرِحٌ—extremely overjoyed—and فَخُورٌ—intensely proud and arrogant.

Here's the tragedy: the difficulty didn't humble him. Instead, it made him depressed and therefore disbelieving. And when the favor came, it didn't turn him back to Allah ﷻ. It just made him celebrate and become arrogant. He takes credit for overcoming his troubles, proud that he made it through, forgetting Who granted him relief.

translation: Except for those who are patient and do righteous deeds; those will have forgiveness and great reward
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ

Allah ﷻ now presents the exception: إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟—"Except those who exercised patience." Notice the sequence: Allah ﷻ mentions صَبَرُوا۟ first, then وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ (they did righteous deeds). This reveals a profound secret about human behavior: without patience, circumstances will dictate your actions.

When life is good, you become so busy enjoying yourself that you have no time for prayer. When life is difficult, you become so angry that you stop praying altogether. Your deeds become dependent on your emotional state. But those who maintain patience—these are the people who control their emotions in both good times and bad, and they continue performing righteous deeds regardless of their circumstances.

This is the true test: can you maintain your relationship with Allah ﷻ when everything is falling apart? Can you maintain it when everything is going perfectly? Those who pass this test—أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ—"those will have forgiveness and a huge compensation."

translation: Then would you possibly leave [out] some of what is revealed to you, or is your breast constrained by it because they say, "Why has there not been sent down to him a treasure or come with him an angel?" But you are only a warner. And Allah is Disposer of all things
commentary:
فَلَعَلَّكَ تَارِكٌۢ بَعْضَ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَضَآئِقٌۢ بِهِۦ صَدْرُكَ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ جَآءَ مَعَهُۥ مَلَكٌ ۚ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ نَذِيرٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ

Allah ﷻ now addresses the Messenger ﷺ directly with a profound rhetorical question: "Is it perhaps the case that you're going to abandon some of what has been revealed to you?" The ayah continues: وَضَآئِقٌۢ بِهِۦ صَدْرُكَ—"and your chest is becoming tight because of it."

This describes a very real social pressure. Sometimes Muslims are put on the spot about their beliefs, forced to defend what seems politically incorrect or socially awkward. Imagine addressing a group of non-Muslims who ask pointed questions: "Do you really believe everyone else is going to hell? Why do you always compare yourselves to other religions? You say Adam and Eve both ate from the tree equally, but Christians believe Eve was primarily at fault—doesn't that make us look bad?"

These confrontational moments test whether you will hold firm to what Allah ﷻ has revealed, even when it makes others uncomfortable. The ayah acknowledges this pressure: people demand, "How come no treasure came down upon him? Or why didn't an angel come with him?"

Allah's response to His Messenger ﷺ is simple yet profound: إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ نَذِيرٌ—"You are only a warner." Not even مُنذِر, but simply نَذِيرٌ—your job is to warn, to give the message clearly. وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ—"And Allah is in complete control, managing all affairs." The word وَكِيلٌ means someone to whom everything is entrusted. Allah ﷻ has left nothing in anyone else's hands—He manages everything Himself.

translation: Or do they say, "He invented it"? Say, "Then bring ten surahs like it that have been invented and call upon [for assistance] whomever you can besides Allah, if you should be truthful
commentary:
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ مُفْتَرَيَـٰتٍ وَٱدْعُوا۟ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

After demanding physical miracles like treasures and angels, the disbelievers shift their criticism: "Or are they saying he fabricated it?" This is a different challenge altogether—they're claiming the Quran itself is made up.

Allah ﷻ responds with a direct challenge: قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ مُفْتَرَيَـٰتٍ—"Tell them: Then bring ten surahs like it, made up just as you claim this one is made up." The word مُفْتَرَيَـٰتٍ turns their accusation back on them. If you're saying this is fabricated, and the Prophet ﷺ doesn't even have a background in poetry while you are experts in the Arabic language, then it shouldn't be difficult for you. Go ahead, fabricate something similar.

وَٱدْعُوا۟ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ—"And call upon whoever you can besides Allah." Get your experts together. Make it a group project. If human beings and jinn gathered together, they still couldn't produce something like it. إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ—"If in fact your claims are truthful."

It's important to note that this ayah appears in what scholars classify as late Makkan revelation. Some scholars divide the Makkan Quran into three periods: early, middle and late. The earliest revelations appear at the end of the Quran, the latest Makkan surahs appear toward the beginning, and the middle Makkan revelations are in the middle of the mushaf. The subject matter corresponds to these periods, with the earliest revelations emphasizing the afterlife most intensely.

translation: And if they do not respond to you - then know that the Qur'an was revealed with the knowledge of Allah and that there is no deity except Him. Then, would you [not] be Muslims
commentary:
فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ بِعِلْمِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَن لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ

Notice Allah ﷻ doesn't say "if they don't respond"—He says فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ—"if they haven't been able to respond to you." The phrasing indicates a track record has already been established. By this late stage of the Makkan period, their inability to meet the challenge is already evident in their behavior.

فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ بِعِلْمِ ٱللَّهِ—"Then you all had better know that it has only been revealed by means of Allah's knowledge." This Quran came with divine knowledge embedded in it. وَأَن لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ—"And that there is no one to be worshiped or obeyed in any way, shape or form except Him."

Then comes the penetrating question: فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ—"Then are you going to give yourselves up? Are you going to submit?"

translation: Whoever desires the life of this world and its adornments - We fully repay them for their deeds therein, and they therein will not be deprived
commentary:
مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فِيهَا وَهُمْ فِيهَا لَا يُبْخَسُونَ

Allah ﷻ now addresses a fundamental obstacle to submission: people who refuse to surrender to Him because they believe submission means sacrificing a good life in this world. But Allah ﷻ has already addressed this concern earlier in the surah, where He promised a good life here and in the hereafter for those who maintain taqwa.

مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا—"Whoever wants worldly life and its decoration"—someone who has decided this is all there is. نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فِيهَا—"We will compensate them in full for their deeds in it." Their investments will pay off. They'll make their money. They'll achieve their worldly success.

وَهُمْ فِيهَا لَا يُبْخَسُونَ—"And they will not be given any less in it." The word يُبْخَسُونَ comes from بَخْس, which means to exchange something for something inferior or less valuable. In the days of barter trade, if someone gave you quality rice and you gave them rotten bananas in return, that would be bakhs—giving less than what's deserved. Allah ﷻ says these people won't be shortchanged in this life. They'll get exactly what they worked for here.

But notice the sarcasm embedded in this phrase. بَخْس is typically used when someone receives less than they deserve and they complain about it. By saying "they won't be given less," Allah ﷻ is speaking about their punishment in the hereafter—they won't receive any less punishment than they earned.

translation: Those are the ones for whom there is not in the Hereafter but the Fire. And lost is what they did therein, and worthless is what they used to do
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَحَبِطَ مَا صَنَعُوا۟ فِيهَا وَبَـٰطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا ٱلنَّارُ—"Those are the people who have nothing in the afterlife except the Fire."

وَحَبِطَ مَا صَنَعُوا۟ فِيهَا—"And everything they manufactured in it has been nullified." The verb صَنَعُوا۟ is significant. It's synonymous with عَمِلَ (to do), but صَنَعَ implies taking time to manufacture something, to build it piece by piece. You construct one part, then another, then assemble them together. It's a process. These people took time building their investments, their homes, their careers, their legacies—but all of it حَبِطَ, utterly nullified.

وَبَـٰطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ—"And everything they used to work toward is batil." The word بَـٰطِلٌ doesn't just mean false—it means something with no weight, no measure, no substance. It's completely without reality.

This is one of the Quran's powerful images of the afterlife. For believers, good deeds have weight and substance. For disbelievers, their deeds—even what appeared to be good—have no weight at all. The Quran describes people whose scales are made light because the deeds they thought were good actually have no substance.

There's an important distinction here between two types of bad deeds. First, there are deeds that are evil in themselves—murder, theft, oppression. Second, there are apparently good deeds performed with corrupt intentions—charity given for reputation, religious leadership pursued for power and recognition. Since the word عَمِل is typically used in a positive sense unless qualified otherwise, some scholars understand this ayah as referring to good deeds that appear outwardly righteous but lack the foundation of proper intention.

Even a person who doesn't believe in the afterlife will do some good deeds. They'll establish charitable foundations and humanitarian organizations. They'll help others in visible ways. But Allah ﷻ says those good deeds may bring benefit in this world, but in the akhirah they are batil—without substance—because they weren't built on the foundation of iman.

The Quran presents a striking image: a disbeliever comes before Allah ﷻ with what he thinks are good deeds, shaking with fear as he presents them. But Allah ﷻ makes them like scattered dust. They have no weight, no reality whatsoever.

translation: So is one who [stands] upon a clear evidence from his Lord [like the aforementioned]? And a witness from Him follows it, and before it was the Scripture of Moses to lead and as mercy. Those [believers in the former revelations] believe in the Qur'an. But whoever disbelieves in it from the [various] factions - the Fire is his promised destination. So be not in doubt about it. Indeed, it is the truth from your Lord, but most of the people do not believe
commentary:
أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ وَيَتْلُوهُ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْهُ وَمِن قَبْلِهِۦ كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ فَٱلنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُۥ ۚ فَلَا تَكُ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

This ayah reveals the fundamental difference in attitude between those who accept revelation and those who reject it. On the surface, good deeds might be performed by both believer and disbeliever, but their internal relationship with truth differs entirely.

أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ—"As for the one who was on clear proof from his Master." The scholars agree almost unanimously that this بَيِّنَة—this clear proof—refers to the fitrah, the natural disposition built into every human being. This person was committed to that innate understanding of truth and didn't allow that decency programmed within him to become corrupted.

وَيَتْلُوهُ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْهُ—"And a witness follows along with it." This witness is revelation itself, specifically the Quran, which comes as شَاهِدٌ, a witness testifying to truth. It comes after the internal proof, following it and confirming it. In legal testimony, you need both evidence and witnesses. Here, the evidence exists internally in the fitrah, and the witness comes externally through revelation. Both elements come together.

Some scholars say this describes the Messenger ﷺ directly and by extension all believers, but primarily the Prophet ﷺ himself. He has bayyinah within him, and then the proof—the witness—comes to him from outside.

وَمِن قَبْلِهِۦ كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً—"And before it came the Book of Musa as a leader and mercy." The word إِمَامًا means a leader, but it also carries the meaning of something that makes you look ahead. The Book of Musa (AS) was designed to make people look forward, to anticipate the final messenger. This was embedded in previous revelation. What were they looking ahead toward? The ultimate mercy—the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ—"Those are the ones who believe in it." Allah ﷻ begins by describing the Messenger ﷺ, then transitions to describe all believers. This process of internal proof confirmed by external witness applies to everyone who truly believes.

وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ فَٱلنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُۥ—"And whoever disbelieves in it from among the groups, then the Fire is his promised destination." Why mention الْأَحْزَابِ (the groups, the factions)? Because by late Makkan revelation, interaction was beginning between Muslims and various groups—Jews, Christians and different sects within each tradition. The Book of Musa (AS) is mentioned because it would have little relevance to the Makkan polytheists, but significant relevance to some among the People of the Book who held it sacred.

فَلَا تَكُ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّنْهُ—"Then you should not be in any doubt whatsoever about it." The word مِرْيَةٍ describes doubt about something that is absolutely certain. It's doubt planted through continuous criticism and questioning, attempting to shake what should be firm conviction.

إِنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ—"It is the ultimate truth from your Master, without any doubt."

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ—"However, most people will not believe."

This final statement addresses a question people often ask: if this is the truth, why aren't most people believing it? If it's really that good, shouldn't most people accept it? It's like asking why, if your product is so superior, so few people are buying it.

Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ—and through him, all believers—that the number of people accepting Islam will never be a measure of its quality. Most people are not going to believe, and that's not a judgment on the message. That's simply the state of humanity. They're not ready to submit.

When people gather together, mob mentality takes over. "The will of the people" becomes the ruling principle, not the will of Allah ﷻ. Modern concepts of individual freedom have essentially turned each person into their own small deity. The ultimate authority over an individual has become the self. "I am my own master. I answer to no one." This is the essence of the contemporary mindset.

Most people will not accept a higher authority. They simply won't do it. This is the reality Allah ﷻ declares, not as a command from us, but as His verdict on the nature of humanity.

translation: And who is more unjust than he who invents a lie about Allah? Those will be presented before their Lord, and the witnesses will say, "These are the ones who lied against their Lord." Unquestionably, the curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers
commentary:
**وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَقُولُ ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ أَلَا لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ**

Who could be a worse wrongdoer than someone who fabricates lies against Allah ﷻ? This rhetorical question comes directly after the previous passage established three forms of proof for the truth: the natural disposition (fitrah) within every person, the revelation that followed, and previous scripture that confirmed it. Despite all these evidences converging, if someone still attributes lies to Allah ﷻ, how much more indecent can a person possibly be?

The ayah declares: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ - these people will be presented before their Master. Notice the passive construction here - they don't want to come before their Master willingly. Rather, they are made to stand before Him. The passive form (yu'radoon) carries an element of coercion; they are forced to appear before Allah ﷻ for judgment.

Then the witnesses will declare: هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ - "These are the ones who lied against their Master." The word هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ is used for pointing to something close by, not far away. When you point at someone from a distance, there might be confusion about who you're indicating. But when you point to someone right in front of you, there's no ambiguity whatsoever. It's as though the witnesses are standing directly before these liars, pointing them out with absolute certainty: "These are the people who did it. No doubt about it."

Also notice that Allah ﷻ is referred to here as رَبِّهِمْ (their Master), not just الله. This emphasizes the relationship of lordship they violated - the One who created them, sustained them, and provided for them, yet they lied against Him.

The ayah concludes with a decisive pronouncement: أَلَا لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - "Beware! The curse of Allah ﷻ falls upon the wrongdoers." The word ظَالِم is crucial here. This term will become the defining characteristic used to describe the nation of Nuh (AS) and what ultimately made them deserving of punishment. Dhulm means much more than simple wrongdoing. It encompasses oppression, committing evil, and placing something where it doesn't belong. These people should have accepted the truth, but instead they wronged themselves and others by rejecting it. They were evil in the deepest sense of the word dhulm.

translation: Who averted [people] from the way of Allah and sought to make it [seem] deviant while they, concerning the Hereafter, were disbelievers
commentary:
**ٱلَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ**

This ayah provides further description of these wrongdoers: ٱلَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - those who obstruct from the path of Allah ﷻ. The verb يَصُدُّونَ is in the present tense, indicating continuous action. They are actively, persistently blocking people from Allah's path - not just themselves, but others as well.

وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا - and they pursue crookedness in it. They're trying to find criticisms, something to say against the message, some way to twist it or find fault with it.

Here's something remarkable about the structure of this passage: while the previous ayah spoke about the future Day of Judgment, Allah ﷻ now switches back to the present tense. He's saying, "This punishment I'm describing - where believers will testify against disbelievers who lied - isn't just some distant future event. It's talking about you right now, about what you're up to at this very moment."

وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ - and particularly when it comes to the Afterlife, they are in denial. They disbelieve. Why is the Akhirah specifically highlighted here, rather than mentioning all of revelation or all of faith? Because when warnings are given, the fundamental warning concerns the Afterlife. If there's one thing they're not taking seriously, it's the Akhirah. And that's precisely why the warning isn't working on them.

translation: Those were not causing failure [to Allah] on earth, nor did they have besides Allah any protectors. For them the punishment will be multiplied. They were not able to hear, nor did they see
commentary:
**أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۘ يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۚ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يُبْصِرُونَ**

This passage echoes themes from the previous Surah Yunus: لَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - these people were never going to overpower anything in the earth. The phrasing لَمْ يَكُونُوا doesn't just mean "they won't be." It carries the meaning of "it was never, ever going to be the case." It was never going to happen that they would overpower the message of Allah ﷻ in the land.

وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ - and they were never going to have any protective friends besides Allah ﷻ. Ever. That was never going to be possible.

يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ - the punishment will be multiplied over them, doubled for them.

Then comes a sarcastic observation: مَا كَانُوا۟ يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يُبْصِرُونَ - they weren't capable of hearing, and they weren't able to see. It's as though Allah ﷻ is saying with irony: "They did have the ability to hear and see, but they refused to use it. And who did they ultimately harm through this refusal?"

translation: Those are the ones who will have lost themselves, and lost from them is what they used to invent
commentary:
**أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ**

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ - those are the people who harmed, who actually bankrupted their own selves. They brought loss upon themselves.

وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ - and everything they used to fabricate is lost upon them. Notice how the tables have turned. The primary allegation against the Quran was افْتَرَىٰ - "He made it up himself. This is fabricated." Now Allah ﷻ reverses the accusation: "This is what you made up, and everything you fabricated is going to be lost on you. Your false gods, your false beliefs, your lies - all of it will vanish when you need it most."

translation: Assuredly, it is they in the Hereafter who will be the greatest losers
commentary:
**لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ**

لَا جَرَمَ - this phrase requires some explanation. The word جَرَمَ in Arabic comes from a root meaning "to cut." It evolved into Persian and Urdu languages as "jurm," meaning crime. Sometimes, influenced by these mixed language interpretations, it gets translated as "crime" in Quranic commentary as well. But the original Arabic meaning of لَا جَرَمَ is more precisely: "there is absolutely no doubt" or "without question."

More than just expressing certainty, لَا جَرَمَ conveys that there is no opposition to this reality. When you talk about something that will happen in the future, there are usually obstacles in its path. But when you say لَا جَرَمَ, it's as though you're declaring: "There are no obstacles whatsoever. Nothing stands in the way of this. Not only is it going to happen - there's nothing that can stop it." It's an unstoppable, inevitable truth.

This phrase appears three times in the Quran: once here and twice in Surah An-Nahl. Structurally, لَا جَرَمَ is always followed by أَنَّ - it's a consistent grammatical pattern.

لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ - without any doubt whatsoever, these people in the Afterlife particularly are going to be the worst of the losers.

We need to understand the difference between خَاسِر (a loser) and الْأَخْسَرُونَ (the worst losers, the most lost). Why are these people specifically described as the worst losers? Because of the layers upon layers of evidence they rejected. There are three coverings, one on top of the other: the fitrah (natural disposition), ultimate revelation, and confirmation from previous Scripture. 

But it doesn't stop there. On top of rejecting all this evidence, they went further: they obstructed not just themselves but others from Allah's path. And that wasn't enough either - they had to search for crookedness and criticisms against the message. When they were given reminders, it's as though they couldn't even hear (مَا كَانُوا۟ يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ). And then they fabricated lies against Allah ﷻ.

All of these transgressions combined - you simply cannot be a bigger loser than this. That's why they're described as الْأَخْسَرُونَ - the worst, most complete losers.

translation: Indeed, they who have believed and done righteous deeds and humbled themselves to their Lord - those are the companions of Paradise; they will abide eternally therein
commentary:
**إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَأَخْبَتُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ**

After describing the worst losers, Allah ﷻ now presents the contrast: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - those who truly believed and did righteous deeds, the good deeds expected of them.

وَأَخْبَتُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ - and they found peace, they turned towards their Master in tranquility. The word أَخْبَتُوا comes from خَبْت, which refers to soft, low-lying land that descends gently - the kind of sandy earth you find by a beach. When rain falls on such land, it absorbs the water peacefully.

Imagine the difference between falling on concrete or rock versus falling on sand. When you fall on something hard, there's resistance - the earth pushes back, and if you fall too hard, you get hurt. But when you fall onto sand, it simply receives you. You sink into it. There's no impact, no resistance. Just absorption and rest.

This is the image behind أَخْبَتُوا. When a tired traveler reaches soft sand and just drops to their knees to relax, sinking into the earth - that's akhbata. It's used when someone turns towards Allah ﷻ in complete peace, like someone exhausted finally plopping themselves down on comfortable ground and finding instant relief.

A modern example: imagine walking a long distance to reach the coast. When you finally arrive at the beach, you just plop yourself down on the sand. That moment of complete relaxation, that peace - that's a physical manifestation of what akhbata means spiritually.

So these are people who truly believed, performed righteous deeds, and found that deep spiritual peace by turning towards their Master. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ - those are the companions of Paradise. هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ - in which they will remain forever.

Some commentators note that وَأَخْبَتُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ might allude to us sitting in peace before Allah ﷻ during our prayers, experiencing that tranquility in His presence. Those who find that peace in this life will be the people of Paradise in the next.

translation: The example of the two parties is like the blind and deaf, and the seeing and hearing. Are they equal in comparison? Then, will you not remember
commentary:
**۞ مَثَلُ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ كَٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْأَصَمِّ وَٱلْبَصِيرِ وَٱلسَّمِيعِ ۚ هَلْ يَسْتَوِيَانِ مَثَلًا ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ**

Now Allah ﷻ presents a powerful comparison: مَثَلُ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ - the example of these two groups - كَٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْأَصَمِّ وَٱلْبَصِيرِ وَٱلسَّمِيعِ - is like the blind and the deaf versus the one who can see and the one who can hear.

هَلْ يَسْتَوِيَانِ مَثَلًا - are these two equal as an example? Can anyone possibly argue that a person who is both blind and deaf is the same as someone who can both see and hear? Nobody would make such an absurd claim. Nobody would argue that these two are capable of the same things or have the same experience of reality.

أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ - then why don't you put even a little bit of effort into remembering? Why don't you reflect?

The point is profound: the difference between truth and falsehood has now become so obvious, so clear, that it's like the difference between someone who is blind and deaf versus someone who can see and hear. If you can recognize that obvious difference in the physical realm, then you should be able to recognize the equally obvious difference between guidance and misguidance, between accepting the truth and rejecting it.

The truth has been made this clear. The evidence has been presented this plainly. So why the resistance? Why the refusal to reflect?

translation: And We had certainly sent Noah to his people, [saying], "Indeed, I am to you a clear warner
commentary:
**وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنِّى لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ**

With this ayah, the narrative of Nuh (AS) begins. He is the first messenger specifically mentioned in this series of prophetic stories in Surah Hud.

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦٓ - and We had certainly sent Nuh to his nation. Notice the past tense and the emphasis (لَقَدْ) - no doubt about it, We definitely sent him.

إِنِّى لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ - "Indeed, I am a clear warner to you." He didn't say نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ (a warner and a bearer of good news). The emphasis throughout these messenger stories is on the aspect of warning - نَذِير.

This connects back to the beginning of Surah Hud, where the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was given both titles: نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ (warner and bearer of good news). But now, as we examine the stories of previous messengers, the focus narrows to their role as warners, as a complement and parallel to our Messenger's ﷺ mission.

The entire summary of revelation is being revisited. We're going back to the basics, back to the fundamental message that echoes across all of human history.

translation: That you not worship except Allah. Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a painful day
commentary:
**أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ**

أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ - that you should not worship, not enslave yourselves to anyone except Allah ﷻ. This is the core message, the fundamental truth that every prophet came to establish.

إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ - I am indeed afraid for you of the punishment of a painful day. Notice the word choice: أَلِيمٍ (painful). Earlier in the surah, when addressing the wrongdoers in general, Allah ﷻ said: إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِى عَذَابٍ مُّقِيمٍ - "Indeed, the wrongdoers are in an enduring punishment" (using مُّقِيمٍ). Now, in Nuh's (AS) warning, the adjective shifts to أَلِيمٍ - painful.

These parallel wordings are not accidental. The purpose is to show history repeating itself. What is happening now to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and his people happened before. Way back, before even recorded history - the only record of which is revelation itself - the same conversation took place.

Think about it: what non-sacred source do we have for the history of Nuh (AS)? What do we know about what he actually said to his people? We only have what Allah ﷻ preserved in revelation. The history that only Allah ﷻ Himself recorded now parallels the conversation happening between the Prophet ﷺ and his people. SubhanAllah.

translation: So the eminent among those who disbelieved from his people said, "We do not see you but as a man like ourselves, and we do not see you followed except by those who are the lowest of us [and] at first suggestion. And we do not see in you over us any merit; rather, we think you are liars
commentary:
**فَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ مَا نَرَىٰكَ إِلَّا بَشَرًا مِّثْلَنَا وَمَا نَرَىٰكَ ٱتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ أَرَاذِلُنَا بَادِىَ ٱلرَّأْىِ وَمَا نَرَىٰ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍۭ بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كَـٰذِبِينَ**

فَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ - Then the chieftains, those who disbelieved from his nation, said...

The word الْمَلَأُ has two related meanings. First, it refers to those who "fill" the important positions - who fill the tribunal, the courthouse, the congress, the parliament, the king's court. These are the people in the seats of power. Second, it refers to those who are themselves "filled" - full of wealth, full of power, full of status. Hence: the chieftains, the elite.

Their response drips with condescension: مَا نَرَىٰكَ إِلَّا بَشَرًا مِّثْلَنَا - "We don't see you as anything but flesh and bone just like us." The word بَشَرًا comes from بَشَرَة, which means skin. "You've got skin like we do. What's so special about you? You're just a human being like us. You're mortal. Why should we follow you?"

وَمَا نَرَىٰكَ ٱتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ أَرَاذِلُنَا - "And we don't see anyone following you except أَرَاذِلُنَا - our lowliest ones."

The word أَرَاذِلُ is the plural of أَرْذَل, which itself is the superlative form of رَذْل. Something radhl is ugly, despicable, inferior - something you want nothing to do with. When you see a certain type of indecent person, you want to get away from them. When you see a particular crowd hanging on a street corner and you choose to walk down a different street because you don't even want to go near those kinds of people - not because you're necessarily afraid, but because you think that's a lowly crowd you wouldn't want to be associated with - that's the essence of radhl.

By using the superlative أَرَاذِل, they're saying: "Socially speaking, these people are beneath every other status that could possibly exist. There's no lower status. They're the scum of the earth. They're riff raff."

The very use of this word expresses their desire to distance themselves. This is the condescending language beginning to emerge - the same pattern we see throughout prophetic history.

بَادِىَ ٱلرَّأْىِ - This phrase is particularly revealing. بَادِىَ means "at first glance" or "immediately apparent." الرَّأْىِ means "opinion" or "view." Together, بَادِىَ ٱلرَّأْىِ means they came to their conclusion immediately, without any real consideration or thought.

When you look at somebody and pass judgment in a split second - you've got them all figured out instantly, and you don't need to reconsider your stereotype because you've already decided who they are - that's بَادِىَ ٱلرَّأْىِ. It's the process of coming very quickly to a conclusion based on what you see immediately, based on what your social environment requires you to think.

The elite already know their own crowd. If they don't recognize you, that means you're insignificant. Think about exclusive restaurants or country clubs - the person at the door already knows all the regular members. But when someone unfamiliar walks in, they're immediately questioned: "Excuse me, sir, are you sure you're at the right place? Do you have proper authorization? Otherwise it's full, and we'll send you on your way." You don't belong in that elite setting.

These chieftains saw Nuh (AS) and his followers and, without spending any time considering the message, immediately judged: "He's just a human" and "his followers are lowly." They didn't take any time to reflect. And once they made that judgment, they didn't have to reconsider it. They could justify their continued arrogance without a problem, without giving it a second thought. "These people are insignificant anyway."

وَمَا نَرَىٰ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍۭ - "And we don't see that you people - of all people - have any kind of favor or advantage over us. You don't enjoy any special status above us." There's shock in their tone, as if to say: "You think you have something on us? You think you're better than us?"

بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كَـٰذِبِينَ - "Rather, we just think you're liars. You're fabricating this. You must have some other agenda. You're just trying to manipulate us, to get close to us somehow."

This is their complete dismissal: You're nobody special, your followers are the lowest of society, you have no advantage over us, and we think you're simply lying about everything.

And so the pattern established at the beginning of this passage - the worst wrongdoing being to fabricate lies against Allah ﷻ - now manifests in the actual historical account. The accusation of lying is thrown at the prophet, when in reality, it is the accusers themselves who are engaged in the greatest lie of all.

translation: He said, "O my people have you considered: if I should be upon clear evidence from my Lord while He has given me mercy from Himself but it has been made unapparent to you, should we force it upon you while you are averse to it
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَءَاتَىٰنِى رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوهَا وَأَنتُمْ لَهَا كَـٰرِهُونَ

Prophet Nuh (AS) begins His response to His people with a powerful rhetorical question: "O my people, have you considered if I am upon clear evidence from my Lord?" The word بَيِّنَة refers to crystal-clear proof, evidence so compelling that it leaves no room for doubt. This is the foundation upon which Nuh (AS) stands—not personal opinion or cultural tradition, but divine certainty from His Master.

But Allah ﷻ did not stop there. Nuh (AS) continues: وَءَاتَىٰنِى رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ—"And He gave me mercy from Himself." This mercy is the revelation itself, a special gift that comes directly from Allah ﷻ. First comes the clear proof, and layered upon that comes divine revelation. These are two distinct blessings that establish the prophet's authority and mission.

Then comes a remarkable shift in pronouns. When Nuh (AS) speaks of what Allah ﷻ has given him personally, he uses the singular: "He gave me." But notice what happens next: فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوهَا—"But it has been made blind to you—should we compel you to accept it?" The pronoun suddenly shifts to "we." Who is this "we"? Nuh (AS) is now speaking on behalf of himself and all the believers.

This is profound. At the beginning of the conversation, he called His followers insignificant in the eyes of the elite. But the moment he begins articulating the message, he unites himself with them in a single pronoun. He's telling the elite: "Let me be clear with you. When it comes to what Allah ﷻ has given me of revelation, that separates me from you. But when it comes to the work of dawah, my followers and I are united. We are together in this."

The ayah ends with another penetrating question: أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوهَا وَأَنتُمْ لَهَا كَـٰرِهُونَ—"Should we force it upon you while you are averse to it?" The word إِلْزَام comes from لَزَمَ, meaning to stick something onto something else, like forcing a sticker onto a surface. "Do you think we're desperate to slap this message onto you? That we want to compel you to accept what you clearly hate?" This echoes what Allah ﷻ told the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in previous surahs: "Are you going to force people to believe?" The answer is no—the message is offered with clarity, but never with coercion.

translation: And O my people, I ask not of you for it any wealth. My reward is not but from Allah. And I am not one to drive away those who have believed. Indeed, they will meet their Lord, but I see that you are a people behaving ignorantly
commentary:
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ لَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مَالًا ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَـٰكِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُمْ قَوْمًا تَجْهَلُونَ

Nuh (AS) addresses the underlying suspicion of the elite: they assume that religious figures always come with hidden financial agendas. "O my people, I do not ask you for any wealth for this message." This is critical. The elite were accustomed to religious charlatans who exploited spirituality for profit. But Nuh (AS) dismantles this assumption immediately: إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ—"My reward is only upon Allah ﷻ."

This reminds us of a troubling pattern that persists even today. The only time religious Muslims approach the wealthy elite—those who show no regard for religion, who may even engage in blasphemous behavior—is when they need funding. "We're opening a madrassa, brother. We need your blessed income to shine upon us." But messengers of Allah ﷻ took the opposite approach. From the very beginning, they told the elite: "I don't need your money. My compensation comes from Allah ﷻ alone."

Then Nuh (AS) addresses their second demand: وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟—"And I will never drive away those who believe." The word طَرْد means to shoo someone away, to dismiss them as inferior, to treat them as unwelcome. "Get away from me—you don't belong here." The elite wanted Nuh (AS) to distance himself from the lower-class believers, but he uses the strongest form of negation: مَآ أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ—"I will never, ever be someone who drives them away."

Why not? إِنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ رَبِّهِمْ—"Indeed, they are going to meet their Lord." These people you look down upon, these believers you consider beneath you, they carry an honor you cannot comprehend. They will stand before Allah ﷻ with dignity. How could I possibly reject them?

But then comes the devastating conclusion: وَلَـٰكِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُمْ قَوْمًا تَجْهَلُونَ—"But I see you as a people who are acting ignorantly." The verb تَجْهَلُونَ is in the present continuous form, suggesting ongoing, active ignorance. "You are choosing to be ignorant. You're letting your emotions override your intellect. You're actively engaging in foolishness." This is not a statement about their intelligence—it's a critique of their willful blindness.

translation: And O my people, who would protect me from Allah if I drove them away? Then will you not be reminded
commentary:
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ مَن يَنصُرُنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِن طَرَدتُّهُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ

Nuh (AS) poses a question that cuts to the heart of the matter: "O my people, who would protect me from Allah ﷻ if I drove them away?" The word نَصَرَ implies to  someone needs assistance or help against an enemy. If Nuh (AS) were to reject his followers to appease the elite, he would be making an enemy of Allah ﷻ Himself. "Who among you could save me from His punishment? Who would come to my aid?"

Then he challenges them: أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ—"Will you not then remember?" The verb تَذَكَّرُونَ implies making even a small effort to recall, to reflect, to think things through. "Why don't you people make even a minimal effort to use your minds?"

What we're witnessing here is a pattern among the wealthy and powerful. Not all political leaders or wealthy individuals are the same, but there is a category among them who refuse to engage in self-reflection. They find convenient excuses to reject the message—usually centered around not wanting to associate with certain groups of people. They prioritize social status over truth. And Nuh (AS) is forcing them to confront this reality: "Why won't you think for yourselves?"

translation: And I do not tell you that I have the depositories [containing the provision] of Allah or that I know the unseen, nor do I tell you that I am an angel, nor do I say of those upon whom your eyes look down that Allah will never grant them any good. Allah is most knowing of what is within their souls. Indeed, I would then be among the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَلَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِندِى خَزَآئِنُ ٱللَّهِ وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ وَلَآ أَقُولُ إِنِّى مَلَكٌ وَلَآ أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِىٓ أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَن يُؤْتِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ خَيْرًا ۖ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ إِنِّىٓ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Nuh (AS) now clarifies the boundaries of his mission, addressing potential misconceptions one by one. "I do not say to you that I possess the treasures of Allah ﷻ"—he's not claiming control over divine resources or worldly wealth. "Nor do I know the unseen"—he doesn't claim knowledge of what's hidden from human perception. "Nor do I say that I am an angel"—he's not claiming a nature different from theirs. These are the false claims that charlatans make.

But then comes the most significant clarification: وَلَآ أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِىٓ أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَن يُؤْتِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ خَيْرًا—"And I do not say about those whom your eyes look down upon that Allah ﷻ will never grant them good."

The word تَزْدَرِي comes from the root ز-ر-ي, which means to view someone as utterly insignificant, worthless, having no social status whatsoever. A person who is زَرِيّ is someone who appears on nobody's radar—socially invisible, the kind of person whose absence wouldn't be noticed. Your eyes look at them and see nothing of value. "But I don't determine who receives good from Allah ﷻ based on your social hierarchy."

ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ—"Allah ﷻ knows better what is in their souls." The worth of a person isn't determined by their social class, their wealth, their family lineage, or how they appear to elite eyes. Allah ﷻ sees what you cannot—the sincerity in their hearts, the purity of their intentions, the quality of their faith.

إِنِّىٓ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ—"Indeed, I would then be among the wrongdoers." If Nuh (AS) were to make such a judgment about his followers, dismissing them based on social status, he himself would be committing dhulm—injustice, wrongdoing. This is remarkable. The concept of wrong (dhulm) has been a recurring theme in the story of Nuh (AS), but now it takes on a new dimension. It's not just about disbelief; it would also be wrong for the prophet himself to discriminate against believers based on worldly standards.

translation: They said, "O Noah, you have disputed us and been frequent in dispute of us. So bring us what you threaten us, if you should be of the truthful
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰنُوحُ قَدْ جَـٰدَلْتَنَا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدَٰلَنَا فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

Having exhausted their arguments and been cornered by his logic, the people shift tactics entirely. "O Nuh, you have argued with us and been excessive in your arguing with us." They're frustrated, unable to refute his points, so they resort to accusation: "You talk too much. You've argued with us over and over again. We've heard enough."

Then comes the challenge: فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ—"So bring us what you promise us"—meaning the punishment he's been warning them about. إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ—"If you are really among the truthful." They've stopped criticizing his followers. They've moved beyond questioning his motives. Now they're directly challenging him: "If you're telling the truth, prove it. Bring the punishment."

This is the final stage of rejection—mockery and taunting. When people run out of arguments, they demand miracles on their terms.

translation: He said, "Allah will only bring it to you if He wills, and you will not cause [Him] failure
commentary:
قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ ٱللَّهُ إِن شَآءَ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ

Nuh's (AS) response is calm and absolute: "Only Allah ﷻ will bring it to you, if He wills." The punishment isn't at the prophet's command—it comes according to divine wisdom and timing. The إِن شَآءَ ("if He wills") isn't doubt about whether it will happen; it's an acknowledgment that the timing belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ—"And you will not be able to escape it." The word مُعْجِزِينَ comes from عَجَزَ, meaning to overpower, to render someone powerless. "You won't be able to make Allah ﷻ powerless. You won't escape. You won't outmaneuver the divine decree. When it comes, no fortress will protect you, no strategy will save you."

translation: And my advice will not benefit you - although I wished to advise you - If Allah should intend to put you in error. He is your Lord, and to Him you will be returned
commentary:
وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِىٓ إِنْ أَرَدتُّ أَنْ أَنصَحَ لَكُمْ إِن كَانَ ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ ۚ هُوَ رَبُّكُمْ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ

Nuh (AS) delivers a sobering reality: "And my advice will not benefit you, even if I wanted to advise you, if Allah ﷻ intends to leave you in error." The word نُصْح refers to sincere advice given with the intention of bringing good to someone, when you genuinely wish well for them and counsel them accordingly. When someone advises you to slow down because they see you driving recklessly, that's nush—they want to protect you from harm.

But here's the painful truth: even the most sincere advice (نُصْحِي in the intensified form, showing strong, repeated counsel) becomes powerless when Allah ﷻ has decided to leave someone in their error. إِن كَانَ ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ—"If Allah ﷻ wills to leave you misguided."

The word غَوَى is significant. It means to take someone off the path they were on and lead them on such a tangent that they become completely lost, unable to find their way back. The only option left is to continue wandering further away. When Allah ﷻ allows someone to remain in this state after they've persistently rejected guidance, no amount of sincere advice can penetrate their hearts.

هُوَ رَبُّكُمْ—"He is your Lord"—your Master, the One who controls your affairs. وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ—"And to Him you will be returned." Whether you find the straight path or not, one truth remains absolute: all paths eventually lead back to Allah ﷻ, either for reward or for punishment.

translation: Or do they say [about Prophet Muhammad], "He invented it"? Say, "If I have invented it, then upon me is [the consequence of] my crime; but I am innocent of what [crimes] you commit
commentary:
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ إِنِ ٱفْتَرَيْتُهُۥ فَعَلَىَّ إِجْرَامِى وَأَنَا۠ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ

This ayah marks a shift back to the present moment, addressing the disbelievers of Makkah who were making the same accusation against the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ: "Or do they say he has invented it?" The "it" refers to the Quran itself.

Allah ﷻ instructs His Messenger ﷺ: قُلْ إِنِ ٱفْتَرَيْتُهُۥ فَعَلَىَّ إِجْرَامِى—"Say: If I have invented it, then upon me is my crime." The word إِجْرَام is profound. It comes from the root ج-ر-م, which means to commit a violation of something that was expected of you, to fail in an obligation you were meant to fulfill. If someone fabricates a message and attributes it to Allah ﷻ, that would be the ultimate ijram—a cosmic crime against the trust between creation and Creator.

"If I've done this, the consequence is mine alone." But then comes the distancing: وَأَنَا۠ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ—"And I am innocent of the crimes you commit." The word بَرِيء means completely absolved, having nothing whatsoever to do with something. "I have nothing to do with the violations you're committing—your rejection of truth, your persistence in disbelief, your mockery of the message. That crime is yours, and yours alone."

translation: And it was revealed to Noah that, "No one will believe from your people except those who have already believed, so do not be distressed by what they have been doing
commentary:
وَأُوحِىَ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ أَنَّهُۥ لَن يُؤْمِنَ مِن قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَن قَدْ ءَامَنَ فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ

After centuries of calling his people to Allah ﷻ, Nuh (AS) receives a revelation that must have shattered his heart: وَأُوحِىَ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ أَنَّهُۥ لَن يُؤْمِنَ مِن قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَن قَدْ ءَامَنَ—"And it was revealed to Nuh that none of your people will believe except those who have already believed."
