Imagine the weight of this. After 950 years of tireless dawah—calling them by night and by day, in public and in private, with every approach and strategy—he's told: "No more. No one else is coming." When he looks back at his congregation, he doesn't see a vast multitude. The number is small. And now he knows with certainty: this is it. This is everyone who will ever believe.

فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ—"So do not be distressed by what they have been doing." The word تَبْتَئِسْ comes from بَأْسَاء, which means harshness and discomfort. But when that discomfort reaches such intensity that it produces overwhelming sadness, the verb form shifts to اِبْتِئَاس. This revelation was so difficult for Nuh (AS) to accept, so painful, so hard to swallow that Allah ﷻ had to console him.

"Don't be sad anymore over their mindless actions. Don't lose sleep over them. The time for hoping they'll change is over. Now you have a different task."

translation: And construct the ship under Our observation and Our inspiration and do not address Me concerning those who have wronged; indeed, they are [to be] drowned
commentary:
وَٱصْنَعِ ٱلْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا وَلَا تُخَـٰطِبْنِى فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ

The instruction comes: وَٱصْنَعِ ٱلْفُلْكَ—"And construct the ship." This was no ordinary task. Nuh (AS) had never built a ship before. His nation had never lived near the sea. They had no maritime tradition, no shipbuilding expertise. How would he know where to begin?

بِأَعْيُنِنَا—"Under Our eyes"—meaning under Allah's direct watch. Every step of the construction would be supervised. "Put that plank here. Nail this there. Adjust that board over there." This wasn't a crude raft made of planks tied together. This would be a massive, sophisticated vessel capable of carrying large numbers of people and animals. And Allah ﷻ would guide every detail of its construction.

وَوَحْيِنَا—"And Our inspiration." So Allah ﷻ is watching and inspiring simultaneously. Whenever Nuh (AS) made a mistake, revelation would come: "No, that should go over there."

Then comes a pre-emptive command: وَلَا تُخَـٰطِبْنِى فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟—"And do not address Me concerning those who have wronged." Allah ﷻ knows what's in Nuh's (AS) heart. As he hammers each nail, as he places each board, he sees his people walking by, going about their day. And the thought consumes him: they're all about to die. Every face he sees is a face that will soon be drowned. His heart can't take it. He wants to plead: "Ya Allah, just a few more. Can we save just a few more before it's too late?"

But before he can even voice this prayer, Allah ﷻ tells him: "Don't address Me about them." The time for mercy has passed. إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ—"Indeed, they are to be drowned." The word مُّغْرَقُونَ is in a form that indicates a completed action—"they are already drowned" in the sight of Allah ﷻ. The decree is absolute. There's no reversing it.

This is a different kind of sabr now. All this time, Nuh (AS) had to exercise patience with disbelievers who rejected him. Now he has to exercise patience with the command of Allah ﷻ Himself—watching his nation walk by, knowing their fate is sealed, and being unable to intercede.

translation: And he constructed the ship, and whenever an assembly of the eminent of his people passed by him, they ridiculed him. He said, "If you ridicule us, then we will ridicule you just as you ridicule
commentary:
وَيَصْنَعُ ٱلْفُلْكَ وَكُلَّمَا مَرَّ عَلَيْهِ مَلَأٌ مِّن قَوْمِهِۦ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُ ۚ قَالَ إِن تَسْخَرُوا۟ مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنكُمْ كَمَا تَسْخَرُونَ

The narrative shifts to the present tense: وَيَصْنَعُ ٱلْفُلْكَ—"And he is constructing the ship." Allah ﷻ wants us to visualize this scene unfolding before our eyes. Nuh (AS) is working, building this massive vessel in the middle of a valley, far from any water.

وَكُلَّمَا مَرَّ عَلَيْهِ مَلَأٌ مِّن قَوْمِهِۦ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُ—"And every time the chiefs of his people passed by him, they ridiculed him." The word مَلَأ refers to the elite, the leadership. They walk by and see this old man building a ship in the desert. "Hey Nuh! Going fishing today? Catch anything good?" They crack jokes. They mock him. They treat him like a madman.

Can you imagine his pain? He's building an ark to save whoever would listen, and they're laughing. Tears stream down his face—not because they're mocking him, but because he knows what's coming for them. Every joke they make, every laugh at his expense, brings them closer to their doom. And he can't save them. Allah ﷻ has forbidden him from even asking.

قَالَ إِن تَسْخَرُوا۟ مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنكُمْ كَمَا تَسْخَرُونَ—"He said: If you ridicule us, we will ridicule you just as you ridicule." Notice the shift to "us" and "we." He wasn't the only object of ridicule—his followers were mocked too. And it seems he wasn't as offended by the mockery directed at him personally as he was by the mockery directed at the believers as a group. When they insulted the faithful, he responded on behalf of all of them: "We will have the last laugh, just as you're laughing now."

translation: And you are going to know who will get a punishment that will disgrace him [on earth] and upon whom will descend an enduring punishment [in the Hereafter]
commentary:
فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ

"Soon you will know who will be struck by a punishment that disgraces him." The word يُخْزِيهِ comes from خِزْي, meaning profound humiliation and disgrace. This isn't just physical punishment—it's a punishment that carries shame and dishonor.

وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ—"And upon whom will descend a punishment that remains." The word حَلَّ means to settle down, to take residence. And مُّقِيمٌ means long-lasting, enduring. Sometimes Allah's punishment is swift—it comes and goes in an instant. Sometimes it spans a defined period—seven nights and eight days, as with other nations. But this one? It stays. It settles in. It becomes permanent.

The warning isn't just that punishment is coming. The warning is about two dimensions: the disgrace that comes with it, and its permanence.

translation: [So it was], until when Our command came and the oven overflowed, We said, "Load upon the ship of each [creature] two mates and your family, except those about whom the word has preceded, and [include] whoever has believed." But none had believed with him, except a few
commentary:
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ ٱلتَّنُّورُ قُلْنَا ٱحْمِلْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَوْلُ وَمَنْ ءَامَنَ ۚ وَمَآ ءَامَنَ مَعَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا—"Until when Our command came." The decree of Allah ﷻ arrived—the moment when the believers would be saved and the wrongdoers would face their punishment. This is the culmination of everything that had been building.

وَفَارَ ٱلتَّنُّورُ—"And the oven overflowed." This is powerful imagery. The word فَارَ is used when you put water on the stove with a lid, and you forget about it. The water starts boiling aggressively, the steam fights with the lid, and suddenly it spills over with tremendous force. The word تَنُّور refers to an oven or a valley.

Some scholars explain this literally: the valley where Nuh (AS) lived was surrounded by mountains, possibly near a coastal region. Allah ﷻ created an opening through the mountains, and the ocean began pouring in. Picture water filling a kettle—it starts at the bottom, rises steadily, and then reaches a boil. The flood waters were filling the valley from below while rain poured from above. The situation was reaching its breaking point.

Others interpret this figuratively: the situation between Nuh (AS) and his people had been building like water coming to a boil—small bubbles at first, then bigger ones, then even bigger, until finally it reached a point of no return. "Things have reached a boiling point" is an expression we still use today. That's exactly what happened here.

قُلْنَا ٱحْمِلْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ—"We said: Load onto it of every creature two mates"—a male and a female of every species. This was about preserving life on earth for what would come after the flood.

وَأَهْلَكَ—"And your family"—those closest to him by blood. إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَوْلُ—"Except those against whom the word has already preceded." Even among his own family, there were those who had rejected faith. The decree against them was already established. Blood relations wouldn't save them.

وَمَنْ ءَامَنَ—"And whoever believed." This includes anyone who had accepted the message, regardless of family lineage or social status. Faith, not lineage, determined who would be saved.

وَمَآ ءَامَنَ مَعَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ—"But none had believed with him except a few." After 950 years of dawah, after countless appeals by night and by day, after every approach and every argument, the number who boarded the ark was heartbreakingly small. This is the reality Nuh (AS) had to face: centuries of effort, and only a few were saved.

Yet even a few believers are precious in the sight of Allah ﷻ. And through them, faith would continue. Through them, the message would be preserved. Through them, humanity would have another chance.

translation: And [Noah] said, "Embark therein; in the name of Allah is its course and its anchorage. Indeed, my Lord is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱرْكَبُوا۟ فِيهَا بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ مَجْر۪ىٰهَا وَمُرْسَىٰهَآ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

He said to them, "Board it. In the name of Allah ﷻ is its sailing and its anchoring. Indeed, my Lord is Forgiving and Merciful."

As the floodwaters begin to rise, Nuh (AS) commands his followers to board the ship. The language here is direct and urgent: ٱرْكَبُوا۟ فِيهَا (Load onto it). This is the moment when preparation meets divine decree, when years of building culminate in salvation.

What follows is remarkable: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ مَجْر۪ىٰهَا وَمُرْسَىٰهَآ (In the name of Allah ﷻ is its sailing and its anchoring). The word مَجْرَىٰهَا refers to the ship's flowing or sailing, while مُرْسَىٰهَا refers to its anchoring or settling. Notice the unique pronunciation here—there's a grammatical slanting in how the word is vocalized. This isn't arbitrary. The very pronunciation of مَجْرَىٰهَا mirrors the tilting and swaying of a ship on turbulent waters, leaning this way and that as massive waves strike it. But when we reach مُرْسَىٰهَا, there's no slant—the word itself settles, just as a ship settles when it anchors.

This linguistic choice paints the scene before our eyes. The Quran doesn't just tell us what happened; it makes us feel the violent rocking of the vessel and the relief when it finally comes to rest.

Then comes something unexpected. Nuh (AS) watches the punishment of Allah ﷻ manifest around him—destruction on an unimaginable scale—yet his words are: إِنَّ رَبِّى لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Indeed, my Lord is Forgiving and Merciful). Not wrathful. Not vengeful. In the midst of divine judgment, the prophet speaks of Allah's forgiveness and mercy. This teaches us that even divine punishment is framed by mercy, that Allah's essence remains الغفور الرحيم (the Forgiving, the Merciful) even when justice must be served.

translation: And it sailed with them through waves like mountains, and Noah called to his son who was apart [from them], "O my son, come aboard with us and be not with the disbelievers
commentary:
وَهِىَ تَجْرِى بِهِمْ فِى مَوْجٍ كَٱلْجِبَالِ وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ ٱبْنَهُۥ وَكَانَ فِى مَعْزِلٍ يَـٰبُنَىَّ ٱرْكَب مَّعَنَا وَلَا تَكُن مَّعَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

And it sailed with them through waves like mountains, and Nuh called to his son who was in isolation, "O my son, come aboard with us and do not be with the disbelievers."

The scene intensifies with stunning imagery: وَهِىَ تَجْرِى بِهِمْ فِى مَوْجٍ كَٱلْجِبَالِ (And it sailed with them through waves like mountains). Notice the verb choice—تَجْرِى (it is flowing) is in the present tense, not the past. This makes the scene vivid and immediate, as if we're watching it unfold right now. The ship isn't just moving through water; it's navigating waves described as mountains. This is what we would call a tsunami today—walls of water towering over the vessel.

Then the narrative shifts to something deeply personal. وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ ٱبْنَهُۥ (And Nuh called to his son). In the midst of this catastrophe, a father sees his child in danger. The word used to describe the son's position is masterful: مَعْزِلٍ. This comes from the root meaning to isolate oneself, to go into a corner, to separate from others. Picture the scene: as floodwaters rise, people scramble for safety. Some climb rooftops, others find high ground. The son of Nuh (AS) has found what he thinks is a safe spot—perhaps a cliff, a hill, some elevated corner where he believes the danger won't reach him.

The word مَعْزِل captures this perfectly. It's like someone at a gathering who doesn't want to socialize, so they find that spot between the door and the hallway where no one will notice them. Or the student who sits behind a pillar to avoid being called on. It suggests someone who has deliberately separated themselves, believing they've found security in isolation.

Nuh (AS) calls out with paternal urgency: يَـٰبُنَىَّ ٱرْكَب مَّعَنَا (O my son, come aboard with us). The address يَـٰبُنَىَّ (O my son) is tender and intimate, dripping with a father's concern. And his plea is simple: ride with us. Don't stay isolated. وَلَا تَكُن مَّعَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (And do not be with the disbelievers).

This final phrase carries a profound warning. Nuh (AS) doesn't say "don't be a disbeliever." He says don't be *with* them. Sometimes the danger isn't just in what we believe but in where we position ourselves. Proximity matters. Association matters. His son had physically separated himself from the believers on the ark, and that physical separation reflected a deeper spiritual separation.

translation: [But] he said, "I will take refuge on a mountain to protect me from the water." [Noah] said, "There is no protector today from the decree of Allah, except for whom He gives mercy." And the waves came between them, and he was among the drowned
commentary:
قَالَ سَـَٔاوِىٓ إِلَىٰ جَبَلٍ يَعْصِمُنِى مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ ۚ قَالَ لَا عَاصِمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ ۚ وَحَالَ بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلْمَوْجُ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُغْرَقِينَ

He said, "I will take refuge on a mountain to protect me from the water." He said, "There is no protector today from the decree of Allah ﷻ, except for one He grants mercy." And the waves came between them, and he was among the drowned.

The son's response reveals his fundamental miscalculation: قَالَ سَـَٔاوِىٓ إِلَىٰ جَبَلٍ يَعْصِمُنِى مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ (He said, "I will take refuge on a mountain to protect me from the water"). He's thinking in purely physical terms. Dad has his boat; I have my mountain. You do your thing; I'll do mine. He believes natural barriers can save him from divine decree.

The word يَعْصِمُنِى means "to protect me" or "to shield me"—he thinks the mountain will be his 'isma, his protection. But he's forgotten that true protection comes only from Allah ﷻ.

Nuh's (AS) response cuts through this delusion: لَا عَاصِمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ (There is no protector today from the decree of Allah ﷻ). Notice he doesn't argue about the height of the mountain or the depth of the water. He takes the conversation to its real level: this isn't about water and mountains. This is about أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ (the command of Allah ﷻ). No physical barrier can protect anyone from divine decree.

إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ (Except for one He grants mercy). The only 'isma today, the only protection available, is Allah's mercy. And that mercy is on the ark, not on the mountain.

Then comes the heartbreaking conclusion: وَحَالَ بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلْمَوْجُ (And the waves came between them). The word حَالَ suggests a barrier, an obstacle. Just as they're having this conversation, a massive wave rises up between father and son like a wall. Remember, we've already been told these waves are like mountains. This wave becomes a curtain, cutting off sight, cutting off sound, cutting off any possibility of reaching him.

فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُغْرَقِينَ (And he was among the drowned). The passive construction here is significant—he became from among those who were drowned. His fate is sealed not by his father's inability to save him, but by his own choice to trust in mountains rather than in Allah's mercy.

translation: And it was said, "O earth, swallow your water, and O sky, withhold [your rain]." And the water subsided, and the matter was accomplished, and the ship came to rest on the [mountain of] Judiyy. And it was said, "Away with the wrongdoing people
commentary:
وَقِيلَ يَـٰٓأَرْضُ ٱبْلَعِى مَآءَكِ وَيَـٰسَمَآءُ أَقْلِعِى وَغِيضَ ٱلْمَآءُ وَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ وَٱسْتَوَتْ عَلَى ٱلْجُودِىِّ ۖ وَقِيلَ بُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

And it was said, "O earth, swallow your water, and O sky, withhold." And the water subsided, and the matter was accomplished, and it came to rest on Judi. And it was said, "Away with the wrongdoing people."

This ayah stands as one of the most rhetorically magnificent verses in the entire Quran. There's a famous account of a Christian scholar in the sixth century who prepared a list of critiques against the Quran. On his way to debate Muslim scholars, he happened to pass by a young student running late to class, reviewing his memorization aloud. The child was reciting this very ayah. The scholar heard it once, and that single hearing was enough for him to embrace Islam. The linguistic beauty, the way it captures such a massive event in so few words—it overwhelmed him.

Let's explore why. The ayah begins: وَقِيلَ (And it was said). Notice what's not mentioned—who said it. The passive voice is used, and in Arabic rhetoric, when you use passive voice without identifying the subject, it's because the listener already knows. The earth knows who's commanding it. It doesn't need to be told "Allah ﷻ says." When the command comes, creation recognizes its Lord.

This carries a lesson for us. The earth, with no intelligence as we understand it, recognizes the voice of its Creator. How much more should we recognize His word when we hear it?

Now watch the form of address: يَـٰٓأَرْضُ (O earth). In Arabic, when you want to get someone's attention—someone who's distracted or not listening—you add extra particles. You say يَا أَيُّهَا to wake them up. But the earth is always paying attention. It's always in standby mode, waiting for Allah's command. So there's no need for those extra attention-getters. The simple يَا is enough. The earth is listening.

Contrast this with how you'd speak to someone you respect or someone you need to coax. You'd use gentle words, kind tones. But Allah ﷻ addresses the earth with the directness you'd use with an animal: يَـٰٓأَرْضُ ٱبْلَعِى (O earth, swallow). It's not softened with courtesy. The earth is in such complete submission that no diplomatic language is needed.

ٱبْلَعِى مَآءَكِ (Swallow your water). The verb ابلعي means to swallow, like something going down a throat. And notice the precision: not just "water," but مَآءَكِ (your water). If the command had been to swallow water without that qualifier, the earth might have swallowed everything—the trees, the mountains, the ship, the people. But the command is specific: just your water. The water that came to you from the rain, that belongs to you—take that back. This precision allows for the preservation of life on the ark.

Also, the water is called "your water" because it was the earth's to begin with. Water evaporates, rises to form clouds, returns as rain. It's a cycle. The earth is being told to take back what was already its own.

وَيَـٰسَمَآءُ أَقْلِعِى (And O sky, withhold). The verb أَقْلِعِى means to stop immediately, to cease. It's the word you'd use to tell someone to stop doing something right now—like telling students "Time's up, pens down, stop writing." The rain doesn't taper off gradually; it stops.

The order matters here. First the earth is commanded, then the sky. Why? Because the believers on the ship need the earth to calm down first. That's the immediate priority for their safety. Once the earth begins to absorb the water, then the sky is told to stop.

وَغِيضَ ٱلْمَآءُ (And the water subsided). This verb suggests gradual withdrawal. The water recedes slowly. And this slow recession is crucial—if the water disappeared instantly, the ship would crash. The gradual subsiding allows for a safe landing.

وَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ (And the matter was accomplished). What matter? The destruction of the wrongdoers and the salvation of the believers. Divine decree has been executed.

وَٱسْتَوَتْ عَلَى ٱلْجُودِىِّ (And it came to rest on Judi). The ship settles evenly on Mount Judi. After all that violent tossing on mountain-sized waves, there's finally stillness.

وَقِيلَ بُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (And it was said, "Away with the wrongdoing people"). The word بُعْدًا is a form of curse—it means complete annihilation, utter destruction. And notice again the passive voice: it was said. Who said it? Perhaps Allah ﷻ. Perhaps the angels. Perhaps the believers. Perhaps even the earth and sky themselves. After such comprehensive destruction, all of creation declares: away with those who did wrong.

translation: And Noah called to his Lord and said, "My Lord, indeed my son is of my family; and indeed, Your promise is true; and You are the most just of judges
commentary:
وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ رَّبَّهُۥ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ ٱبْنِى مِنْ أَهْلِى وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنتَ أَحْكَمُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ

And Nuh called upon his Lord and said, "My Lord, indeed my son is of my family, and indeed Your promise is truth, and You are the most just of judges."

The flood has ended. The wrongdoers have perished. But Nuh (AS) is not at peace. A father has watched his son drown, and now he turns to His Lord with a question that reveals his human heart.

وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ رَّبَّهُۥ (And Nuh called upon his Lord). After everything, he doesn't harbor resentment. He doesn't blame Allah ﷻ. He calls upon his Rabb—his Master, his Nurturer—with a genuine question.

فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ ٱبْنِى مِنْ أَهْلِى (My Lord, indeed my son is of my family). The logic seems straightforward: You promised to save me and my family. My son is part of my family. What happened?

وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ ٱلْحَقُّ (And indeed Your promise is truth). Notice the structure—he's not accusing Allah ﷻ of breaking His promise. He's stating emphatically that Allah's promise *is* truth. There's no doubt about that. But there's something he doesn't understand.

وَأَنتَ أَحْكَمُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (And You are the most just of judges). Again, he's not questioning Allah's judgment. He's acknowledging that Allah ﷻ has wisdom beyond his own. But within that acknowledgment is an implied question: Help me understand, because my limited wisdom can't reconcile what I thought was promised with what I've just witnessed.

This is the adab (etiquette) of asking Allah ﷻ. Nuh (AS) doesn't demand answers. He doesn't accuse. He presents his confusion while affirming Allah's perfection, and in doing so, he creates space for divine guidance.

translation: He said, "O Noah, indeed he is not of your family; indeed, he is [one whose] work was other than righteous, so ask Me not for that about which you have no knowledge. Indeed, I advise you, lest you be among the ignorant
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰنُوحُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صَـٰلِحٍ ۖ فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَعِظُكَ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ

He said, "O Nuh, indeed he is not of your family; indeed, he is an unrighteous deed. So do not ask Me that of which you have no knowledge. Indeed, I advise you, lest you be among the ignorant."

Allah's response recalibrates Nuh's (AS) entire understanding: يَـٰنُوحُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ (O Nuh, indeed he is not of your family). This statement redefines family. The biological relationship exists, yes, but that's not what makes someone "family" in the sight of Allah ﷻ.

إِنَّهُۥ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صَـٰلِحٍ (Indeed, he is an unrighteous deed). The phrasing here is extraordinary. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "he committed unrighteous deeds." He says *he is* an unrighteous deed. The son's entire being, his whole personality, had become defined by his rejection of truth. He wasn't just someone who did wrong—he had become wrong itself, embodied. It's like saying if you looked up "unrighteousness" in a dictionary, you'd find his picture.

فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ (So do not ask Me that of which you have no knowledge). This is gentle correction. Allah ﷻ is teaching Nuh (AS) that there are realities beyond his perception. The promise to save his "family" meant those who truly belonged to him through faith, not just blood. This is knowledge Nuh (AS) didn't possess, and Allah ﷻ is directing him not to inquire into matters whose full reality he cannot grasp.

إِنِّىٓ أَعِظُكَ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (Indeed, I advise you, lest you be among the ignorant). The word أَعِظُكَ means to counsel, to advise with deep concern. Allah ﷻ isn't reprimanding harshly; He's protecting His prophet from falling into ignorance. And what is that ignorance? Allowing emotions—even the powerful emotions of parental love—to cloud one's understanding of divine wisdom. It's being cautioned against letting feelings override faith in Allah's perfect judgment.

translation: [Noah] said, "My Lord, I seek refuge in You from asking that of which I have no knowledge. And unless You forgive me and have mercy upon me, I will be among the losers
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّىٓ أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْـَٔلَكَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِى وَتَرْحَمْنِىٓ أَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

He said, "My Lord, I seek refuge in You from asking that of which I have no knowledge. And unless You forgive me and have mercy upon me, I will be among the losers."

Nuh's (AS) response demonstrates the response of a true prophet when corrected by Allah ﷻ. قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّىٓ أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْـَٔلَكَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ (My Lord, I seek refuge in You from asking that of which I have no knowledge). He immediately seeks protection from the very thing he's just been cautioned about. He doesn't defend himself, doesn't make excuses. He takes refuge in Allah ﷻ from his own limitation.

وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِى وَتَرْحَمْنِىٓ أَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (And unless You forgive me and have mercy upon me, I will be among the losers). This echoes the prayer of Adam (AS) after his mistake: رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers) (7:23).

There's a beautiful parallel here. Adam (AS) descended from paradise to earth after his mistake and repentance. Now Nuh (AS), after his mistake and repentance, is about to descend from the ark to restart humanity on earth. He is like a second Adam, and his prayer mirrors the first. Both prophets acknowledge their complete dependence on Allah's forgiveness and mercy.

translation: It was said, "O Noah, disembark in security from Us and blessings upon you and upon nations [descending] from those with you. But other nations [of them] We will grant enjoyment; then there will touch them from Us a painful punishment
commentary:
قِيلَ يَـٰنُوحُ ٱهْبِطْ بِسَلَـٰمٍ مِّنَّا وَبَرَكَـٰتٍ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّمَّن مَّعَكَ ۚ وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ ثُمَّ يَمَسُّهُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

It was said, "O Nuh, disembark with peace from Us and blessings upon you and upon nations from those with you. But other nations We will grant enjoyment; then there will touch them from Us a painful punishment."

The response from Allah ﷻ is filled with mercy: قِيلَ يَـٰنُوحُ ٱهْبِطْ بِسَلَـٰمٍ مِّنَّا (It was said, "O Nuh, disembark with peace from Us"). The word ٱهْبِطْ (descend) is the same word used when Adam (AS) was sent down to earth. This is the second descent, the second beginning for humanity. But notice what accompanies it: بِسَلَـٰمٍ مِّنَّا (with peace from Us). Allah ﷻ sends peace before anything else. The question has been answered, the correction has been made, and now there's only peace.

وَبَرَكَـٰتٍ عَلَيْكَ (And blessings upon you). Not just peace, but بَرَكَـٰتٍ—multitudes of blessings. This is confirmation that Nuh (AS) has been forgiven, that Allah ﷻ is pleased with him.

وَعَلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّمَّن مَّعَكَ (And upon nations from those with you). The people on the ark aren't just individuals—they're described as أُمَمٍ (nations). Each one will become the father or mother of nations. Their descendants will spread across the earth, and among them will be prophets and righteous people. This is especially true of the lineage of Sem (Sam), from whom many prophets descended, including Ibrahim (AS), Musa (AS), Isa (AS), and Muhammad ﷺ.

وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ (But other nations We will grant enjoyment). There will also be nations who receive worldly blessings, comfort, and prosperity. Allah ﷻ will give them much in this life.

ثُمَّ يَمَسُّهُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (Then there will touch them from Us a painful punishment). But if they follow the path of rejection like those who were destroyed in the flood, punishment will come. The cycle of messengers and rejection, guidance and misguidance, will continue throughout human history.

This ayah acknowledges a difficult reality: not all of humanity will choose guidance, even after such a clear lesson. Allah ﷻ will continue to bless people with provision and time, but those who insist on wrongdoing will face consequences.

translation: That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you, [O Muhammad]. You knew it not, neither you nor your people, before this. So be patient; indeed, the [best] outcome is for the righteous
commentary:
تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهَآ إِلَيْكَ ۖ مَا كُنتَ تَعْلَمُهَآ أَنتَ وَلَا قَوْمُكَ مِن قَبْلِ هَـٰذَا ۖ فَٱصْبِرْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْعَـٰقِبَةَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ

That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you. You knew it not, neither you nor your people, before this. So be patient. Indeed, the outcome is for the righteous.

The conclusion ties everything back to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and his mission. تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ (That is from the news of the unseen). The word تِلْكَ (those) is plural, suggesting we should view this account as multiple lessons, not just one story. Every part of Nuh's (AS) experience—the building, the flood, the conversation about his son, the landing—each carries its own wisdom.

نُوحِيهَآ إِلَيْكَ (Which We reveal to you). This isn't information from books or oral tradition. It's وَحْي (revelation), direct from Allah ﷻ.

مَا كُنتَ تَعْلَمُهَآ أَنتَ وَلَا قَوْمُكَ مِن قَبْلِ هَـٰذَا (You knew it not, neither you nor your people, before this). The Prophet ﷺ wasn't there. His people weren't there. There's no way this level of detail could come from human sources. This is proof of the Quran's divine origin.

فَٱصْبِرْ (So be patient). This is the takeaway for the Messenger ﷺ. You're facing rejection like Nuh (AS) faced rejection. You're calling people to truth just as he called people to truth for 950 years. Your own family members are refusing to believe, just as his son refused. So what must you do? Be patient.

إِنَّ ٱلْعَـٰقِبَةَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (Indeed, the outcome is for the righteous). The end belongs to those who have taqwa—those who are conscious of Allah ﷻ, who protect themselves from His displeasure. No matter how long the struggle, no matter how overwhelming the opposition, the ultimate victory is already written for the people of righteousness.

This is the thesis from the story of Nuh (AS): patience in the face of rejection, trust in Allah's wisdom even when you don't understand, and certainty that the final outcome belongs to those who remain conscious of their Lord.

translation: And to 'Aad [We sent] their brother Hud. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. You are not but inventors [of falsehood]
commentary:
وَإِلَىٰ عَادٍ أَخَاهُمْ هُودًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا مُفْتَرُونَ

The narrative shifts to the people of Aad, to whom Allah ﷻ sent their brother Hud (AS). The word order here is significant—notice how "Hud" appears without any introductory phrase. The structure implies an unstated verb: "We sent to Aad their brother Hud." This construction appears multiple times throughout the Quran, establishing a pattern in how these prophetic stories unfold.

The historical progression is important to understand. After nations were destroyed, the believing survivors would migrate to different regions. Their descendants would grow and eventually form new communities. This is how we move chronologically from the people of Nuh (AS) to Aad, then to Thamud, and so forth through the various nations mentioned in this surah.

Hud (AS) begins with the fundamental call that echoes through every prophetic mission: "O my people, enslave yourselves to Allah. You don't have any entity worthy of worship or obedience in any way, shape or form whatsoever except Him."

The phrase مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ (you have no god other than Him) is more emphatic than simply saying "there is no god but Allah." The construction suggests an ongoing argument or debate—Hud (AS) is responding to their claims and justifications for their idolatry.

The ayah concludes with a direct confrontation: إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا مُفْتَرُونَ (You're doing nothing but making things up). This challenges the very foundation of their belief system, declaring their worship to be pure fabrication.

translation: O my people, I do not ask you for it any reward. My reward is only from the one who created me. Then will you not reason
commentary:
يَـٰقَوْمِ لَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِىٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

Hud (AS) preemptively addresses a common objection against messengers: the accusation that they seek personal gain. "I'm not asking you for any form of compensation whatsoever. My compensation rests only with the One who fashioned me and molded me and created me."

The word فَطَرَ is particularly meaningful—it means to originate something, to bring it into existence from nothing. Hud (AS) anchors his trust entirely in his Creator, the One who fashioned him into being.

The ayah ends with a powerful question: أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (Why then do you refuse to use your intellect?). This appeal to reason is characteristic of Quranic argument. If you would simply engage your minds, you would recognize the obvious—that a messenger seeking no worldly compensation must be sincere in his mission.

translation: And O my people, ask forgiveness of your Lord and then repent to Him. He will send [rain from] the sky upon you in showers and increase you in strength [added] to your strength. And do not turn away, [being] criminals
commentary:
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يُرْسِلِ ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْكُم مِّدْرَارًا وَيَزِدْكُمْ قُوَّةً إِلَىٰ قُوَّتِكُمْ وَلَا تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ

Now Hud (AS) presents a positive vision—what they stand to gain through faith. "O my nation, seek forgiveness of your Master, then repent back towards Him." Notice the sequence: استغفار (seeking forgiveness) followed by توبة (repentance). This same pattern appeared in the story of Nuh (AS), teaching us through these historical accounts that reformation begins with acknowledging our wrongs and then turning back to Allah ﷻ.

The promise is tangible and immediate: يُرْسِلِ ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْكُم مِّدْرَارًا (He would send the sky upon you—continuously). The phrase "send the sky" is a beautiful Quranic idiom meaning He will send the best of what the sky has to offer. Just as when the Quran mentions that Adam (AS) ate from "the tree," it means the fruit of the tree—the best it offers. Similarly, the best the sky offers is abundant rain.

The word مِدْرَار comes from the root د-ر-ر. In Arabic, a cow that gives abundant milk is called a durr. An eye that tears profusely is also described with this root. So midraar describes rain that creates an abundance of water and flows continuously—not just a passing shower, but sustained blessing.

Beyond rain, Allah ﷻ promises وَيَزِدْكُمْ قُوَّةً إِلَىٰ قُوَّتِكُمْ (He will increase you with power in addition to the power you already possess). The people of Aad were already known for their physical strength and might, but faith would elevate them even further.

The ayah concludes with a warning: وَلَا تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (And don't turn back, committing crimes). Don't turn away from this truth while violating Allah's expectations and committing offenses against His commands.

translation: They said, "O Hud, you have not brought us clear evidence, and we are not ones to leave our gods on your say-so. Nor are we believers in you
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰهُودُ مَا جِئْتَنَا بِبَيِّنَةٍ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَارِكِىٓ ءَالِهَتِنَا عَن قَوْلِكَ وَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ

The response of his people reveals three layers of rejection. First: مَا جِئْتَنَا بِبَيِّنَةٍ (You haven't brought us anything clear). They claim his message lacks compelling evidence.

Second: وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَارِكِىٓ ءَالِهَتِنَا عَن قَوْلِكَ (We're never going to abandon our gods just based on your word). Their commitment to their idols runs deep—they won't let go regardless of what Hud (AS) says.

Third, and most telling: وَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (And for your sake, we're never going to become believers). The phrase لَكَ (for you/for your sake) is revealing. Despite Hud's clear statement that he seeks no compensation, they still accuse him of having ulterior motives. They suggest he wants them to believe for his own benefit, that he's got "something up his sleeve." This suspicion becomes their excuse for rejection.

translation: We only say that some of our gods have possessed you with evil." He said, "Indeed, I call Allah to witness, and witness [yourselves] that I am free from whatever you associate with Allah
commentary:
إِن نَّقُولُ إِلَّا ٱعْتَرَىٰكَ بَعْضُ ءَالِهَتِنَا بِسُوٓءٍ ۗ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أُشْهِدُ ٱللَّهَ وَٱشْهَدُوٓا۟ أَنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ

Their rejection takes an even darker turn. They say: إِن نَّقُولُ إِلَّا ٱعْتَرَىٰكَ بَعْضُ ءَالِهَتِنَا بِسُوٓءٍ (All we have to say is that some of our gods have afflicted you with evil).

The word ٱعْتَرَىٰ comes from the root ع-ر-ي, which relates to nakedness. It came to mean a sickness so severe that it drives a person to madness—imagine someone becoming so physically sick they lose their mind and wander out naked, hallucinating. For the people of Aad, such illness was associated with demonic possession. They're essentially saying: "You've offended our gods by mocking them, and now they've caused an evil spirit to possess you. You've lost your mind—that's the only explanation for what you're saying."

Hud's response is fearless and unequivocal: إِنِّىٓ أُشْهِدُ ٱللَّهَ وَٱشْهَدُوٓا۟ أَنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ (I am making Allah witness, and you become witnesses yourselves, that I have nothing to do with the associations you make—the shirk you commit).

translation: Other than Him. So plot against me all together; then do not give me respite
commentary:
مِن دُونِهِۦ ۖ فَكِيدُونِى جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ لَا تُنظِرُونِ

The statement continues: مِن دُونِهِۦ (besides Him)—completing the thought that he disassociates from everything they worship other than Allah ﷻ.

Then comes a challenge that demonstrates absolute trust in Allah ﷻ: فَكِيدُونِى جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ لَا تُنظِرُونِ (Go ahead and plot against me, all of you together, then don't wait for me). 

This is remarkable. Hud (AS) tells them: "Have your meeting—make sure nobody misses it. Plan it all out together. Come up with your best scheme. Then execute it against me immediately—don't give me any time to prepare or escape." This level of confidence can only come from complete reliance on Allah ﷻ, which he makes explicit in the next ayah.

translation: Indeed, I have relied upon Allah, my Lord and your Lord. There is no creature but that He holds its forelock. Indeed, my Lord is on a path [that is] straight
commentary:
إِنِّى تَوَكَّلْتُ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ رَبِّى وَرَبِّكُم ۚ مَّا مِن دَآبَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ ءَاخِذٌۢ بِنَاصِيَتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

Hud (AS) explains the source of his fearlessness: إِنِّى تَوَكَّلْتُ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ رَبِّى وَرَبِّكُم (I have placed my complete trust, my reliance, upon Allah—my Master and your Master).

Then he paints a vivid image of Allah's absolute control: مَّا مِن دَآبَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ ءَاخِذٌۢ بِنَاصِيَتِهَآ (Not a single creature walks the earth except that He's got it held by its forehead). The ناصية (forelock, forehead) is the part of an animal's head used to control it—when you hold an animal by its forehead or the top of its head, you control where it goes. Every creature, every person plotting against him, is already in Allah's grip.

The ayah concludes with a profound statement: إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (No doubt about it, my Master is upon a straight path). What does it mean that Allah ﷻ is on a straight path? It means that the only way to truly find Allah ﷻ and serve Him is on this path. There is no other route that leads to Him. My Master can only be found and reached through this straight path of truth He has revealed.

translation: But if they turn away, [say], "I have already conveyed that with which I was sent to you. My Lord will give succession to a people other than you, and you will not harm Him at all. Indeed my Lord is, over all things, Guardian
commentary:
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦٓ إِلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّى قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّونَهُۥ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ

Hud (AS) delivers his final message: فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦٓ إِلَيْكُمْ (If you're going to turn away, then I've already delivered to you, I've completely conveyed to you, whatever I was sent with toward you). His duty as a messenger is fulfilled. The message has been made clear. The choice is now theirs.

Then he reveals a powerful truth about Allah's plan: وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّى قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ (My Master will replace you with another nation). The word يَسْتَخْلِفُ means to leave someone behind to take over affairs—like when a parent leaves the business to their children, or when an elder generation passes stewardship to the next. Allah ﷻ will wipe them out and establish a different people who will take care of the land in their place.

وَلَا تَضُرُّونَهُۥ شَيْـًٔا (And you will not be harming Him in any way, shape or form). Their rejection doesn't hurt Allah ﷻ—they only harm themselves. إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ (No doubt about it, my Master is in complete guardianship—He's guarding every single thing). Everything is under His watchful protection and control.

translation: And when Our command came, We saved Hud and those who believed with him, by mercy from Us; and We saved them from a harsh punishment
commentary:
وَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا هُودًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ

The decree of destruction finally arrives: وَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا (When Our decision finally came). Allah ﷻ uses the royal "We" throughout this passage, emphasizing His majesty and authority.

نَجَّيْنَا هُودًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا (We completely rescued Hud and those who believed along with him, with a special mercy that came from Us). The rescue isn't just physical deliverance—it's accompanied by Allah's special mercy. They're not just saved; they're honored with divine compassion.

وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ (And We rescued them from an intense, tough, rigid, harsh punishment). The word غَلِيظ describes something thick, heavy, severe—a punishment that would have been unbearable.

translation: And that was 'Aad, who rejected the signs of their Lord and disobeyed His messengers and followed the order of every obstinate tyrant
commentary:
وَتِلْكَ عَادٌ ۖ جَحَدُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَصَوْا۟ رُسُلَهُۥ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ كُلِّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ

Allah ﷻ now summarizes the fate of Aad: وَتِلْكَ عَادٌ (And that was Aad). Three charges are laid against them:

First: جَحَدُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ (They argued against, they debated against, they tried to make cases against the miraculous signs of their Master). They didn't just ignore the truth—they actively fought against it.

Second: وَعَصَوْا۟ رُسُلَهُۥ (And they disobeyed His messengers). Notice the plural "messengers" even though they only rejected one messenger—Hud (AS). This is a recurring pattern in the Quran: to disobey one messenger is to disobey them all, because they all come with the same fundamental message. Later in the Quran we're told that denying one is like denying all of them, because they form a unified chain of prophetic guidance.

Third: وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ كُلِّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ (And they followed the command of every tyrannical oppressor). The word جَبَّار describes someone who possesses power and uses it to dominate others. The word عَنِيد comes from the root ع-ن-د, meaning to oppose or go against. An 'aneed is someone who knows what the truth is but can't stand it and deliberately goes the opposite direction. They've "gone off the deep end" and want nothing to do with truth or justice. So the people of Aad followed every tyrant who opposed the way of truth.

translation: And they were [therefore] followed in this world with a curse and [as well] on the Day of Resurrection. Unquestionably, 'Aad denied their Lord; then away with 'Aad, the people of Hud
commentary:
وَأُتْبِعُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ عَادًا كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّعَادٍ قَوْمِ هُودٍ

The consequence spans two worlds: وَأُتْبِعُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً (They were followed in this worldly life by a curse). Their legacy became one of disgrace and expulsion from Allah's mercy. وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (And on the Day of Resurrection) that curse continues—they carry it into the next life as well.

أَلَآ إِنَّ عَادًا كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ (You had better know—no doubt—Aad disbelieved in their Master). The repetition of أَلَا (you had better know) creates emphasis, demanding attention to this critical lesson.

أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّعَادٍ قَوْمِ هُودٍ (You had better know—away with Aad, the nation of Hud). This is particularly striking. Earlier, when the people of Nuh (AS) were destroyed, Allah ﷻ said: "Away with the wrongdoing people" (بُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ), highlighting their wrongdoing as the cause of their destruction. But here, He says "the nation of Hud" (قَوْمِ هُودٍ).

Why? Because "Aad" was actually the name of one of their great leaders, and they named their entire nation after him. Aad became a symbol of their pride and identity. So Allah ﷻ addresses them using the very name they took such pride in: "Away with them and their pride—the nation of Hud." Their source of arrogance becomes the marker of their downfall.

translation: And to Thamud [We sent] their brother Salih. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. He has produced you from the earth and settled you in it, so ask forgiveness of Him and then repent to Him. Indeed, my Lord is near and responsive
commentary:
وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ هُوَ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيهَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى قَرِيبٌ مُّجِيبٌ

The narrative now shifts to the people of Thamud: وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا (And to Thamud, We sent their brother Salih). Like Hud (AS) before him, Salih (AS) begins with the same fundamental call: يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ (O my people, enslave yourselves to Allah. There is absolutely nothing for you—no one worthy of worship or obedience in any way, shape or form—except Him).

But notice how Salih (AS) adds a unique dimension to this call: هُوَ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ (He originated you from the earth). The word أَنشَأَ means to bring something into existence, to raise it up. He created you from nothing, bringing you forth from the earth.

وَٱسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيهَا (And He gave you settlement in it for ages). This word is profound: ٱسْتَعْمَرَ combines the concepts of life span (عُمْر) with establishment (عَمَرَ, to inhabit). He didn't just settle you temporarily—He gave you stability for generations upon generations. You've been given not just a place to live, but an era, an age to flourish in this land.

Then comes the call to action: فَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ (Then seek forgiveness from Him, then repent toward Him). Again, we see this repeated pattern—the same sequence of istighfar and tawbah that appeared in the stories of both Nuh (AS) and Hud (AS). The central themes of Makkan surahs repeat from different mouths, reinforcing the universal message.

إِنَّ رَبِّى قَرِيبٌ مُّجِيبٌ (No doubt about it, my Master is close and He's responsive). This is Salih's unique addition—emphasizing not just Allah's power, but His accessibility. He's near, and He immediately responds to those who seek His forgiveness and turn to Him in repentance. It won't take time. He's already there, already listening, already ready to respond.

translation: They said, "O Salih, you were among us a man of promise before this. Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers worshipped? And indeed we are, about that to which you invite us, in disquieting doubt
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰصَـٰلِحُ قَدْ كُنتَ فِينَا مَرْجُوًّا قَبْلَ هَـٰذَآ ۖ أَتَنْهَىٰنَآ أَن نَّعْبُدَ مَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَإِنَّنَا لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَآ إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ

The response of Thamud echoes the pattern of rejection we've seen before, but with a particularly painful opening: قَدْ كُنتَ فِينَا مَرْجُوًّا قَبْلَ هَـٰذَآ (You used to be one in whom we placed our hopes, before all of this).

This phrase كُنتَ فِينَا مَرْجُوًّا deserves special attention, especially for younger people. The word مَرْجُوّ means someone in whom hopes are placed, someone who shows potential. This is a statement many young Muslims hear when they choose to dedicate themselves to Islamic learning or practice.

"We used to have so much hope in you. You had potential. We said this young person is sharp, going places. They're going to take this family, this community, to a different level. They're going to make us proud. We had all our hopes placed in you. And now what have you done? You want to study Quran? You got accepted into medical school and you're giving it up for what—religious studies? You had a high-paying job in the stock market—how could you leave that? You were doing so well. You're such a huge disappointment."

This is exactly what they're saying to Salih (AS): "You were our pride and joy. What happened to you?"

Then they continue: أَتَنْهَىٰنَآ أَن نَّعْبُدَ مَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا (Are you forbidding us from worshiping what our forefathers worshiped?). Not only are you a disappointment to us now, you're a disgrace to our history, our legacy. This temple was built by your great-grandfather. We've honored these traditions for generations. And you're going to come and mock these gods?

وَإِنَّنَا لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَآ إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ (And we are in such profound doubt about what you're calling us to—it's so confusing). The word مُرِيب is intensive—it means doubt that is so severe it causes doubt in others as well. "Every time you speak, it puts us in even more confusion. Your message is so perplexing that it's making other people doubt too."

translation: He said, "O my people, have you considered: if I should be upon clear evidence from my Lord and He has given me mercy from Himself, who would protect me from Allah if I disobeyed Him? So you would not increase me except in loss
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَءَاتَىٰنِى مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً فَمَن يَنصُرُنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ عَصَيْتُهُۥ ۖ فَمَا تَزِيدُونَنِى غَيْرَ تَخْسِيرٍ

Salih (AS) responds with the same argument we heard from Hud (AS), because the accusation is the same: أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى (Have you considered—what if I'm standing on a clear proof from my Master?). The word بَيِّنَة means something self-evident, a clarity that needs no further explanation.

وَءَاتَىٰنِى مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً (And on top of that, He gave me a special mercy from Him). This is about prophethood itself—a gift, a responsibility, a burden that only Allah ﷻ can help someone carry.

فَمَن يَنصُرُنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ عَصَيْتُهُۥ (Who's going to aid me against Allah if I disobey Him?). "You think I'm doing this for popularity? For your approval? If I know the truth and I disobey Allah ﷻ to please you, who's going to protect me from His punishment?"

فَمَا تَزِيدُونَنِى غَيْرَ تَخْسِيرٍ (In that case, you would not be increasing me in anything except continuous and complete loss). "What hopes did you have in me? The only thing you can offer me now is loss—loss in this life and the next. You're not helping me by asking me to abandon this message. You're only increasing my destruction."

translation: And O my people, this is the she-camel of Allah - [she is] to you a sign. So let her feed upon Allah 's earth and do not touch her with harm, or you will be taken by an impending punishment
commentary:
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ ءَايَةً فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِىٓ أَرْضِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ قَرِيبٌ

Now comes the miraculous sign unique to the story of Thamud: وَيَـٰقَوْمِ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ ءَايَةً (And O my people, this is Allah's she-camel, coming to you as a sign). This isn't just any camel—it's specifically designated as belonging to Allah ﷻ, a divine sign made manifest.

فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِىٓ أَرْضِ ٱللَّهِ (Then leave it alone—let it eat in Allah's land). Notice the emphasis: it's not your land. Don't complain "that's my farm, that's my yard." It's Allah's land. The camel has as much right to it as you do—more, in fact, since it's a sign from the Owner of all land.

وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ (And don't you touch it with evil intent). The word تَمَسُّوهَا means to touch, but combined with بِسُوٓءٍ (with evil), it means don't harm it in any way. Don't lay a hand on it with ill intention.

فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ قَرِيبٌ (For then He will seize you—meaning Allah ﷻ will seize you—with a punishment that is close, imminent). Not "a close punishment will seize you," but "He will seize you with punishment close by." The threat is immediate and personal.

translation: But they hamstrung her, so he said, "Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days. That is a promise not to be denied
commentary:
فَعَقَرُوهَا فَقَالَ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ فِى دَارِكُمْ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ

Despite the clear warning, they crossed the line: فَعَقَرُوهَا (Then they slaughtered it). The word عَقَرَ doesn't just mean to kill—it means to viciously slaughter an animal, particularly by cutting it from underneath the neck and pulling out its insides. It's a brutal, merciless way of killing. They didn't just harm the camel—they massacred it in the most ruthless manner possible.

The immediate response comes through Salih (AS): فَقَالَ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ فِى دَارِكُمْ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ (Then he said to them: Enjoy life in your homes for three days). "Live it up. You've got three days left." 

This is different from the story of Nuh (AS), who was told to build a ship and was given extended time. Here, the countdown is immediate and specific: three days.

ذَٰلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ (That is a promise that will not be denied, that won't be cancelled). The word غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ literally means "not lied against"—it's a promise that cannot and will not be broken. The decree has been made. The time is set. Their fate is sealed.

translation: So when Our command came, We saved Salih and those who believed with him, by mercy from Us, and [saved them] from the disgrace of that day. Indeed, it is your Lord who is the Powerful, the Exalted in Might
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا صَـٰلِحًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَمِنْ خِزْىِ يَوْمِئِذٍ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ

When Our decision came, We rescued Salih (AS) and those who believed along with him through a special mercy from Us, saving them from the humiliation of that particular day. No doubt, your Master is the Ultimately Mighty, the Final Authority.

There's something remarkable about the repetition in these passages. Students who memorize the Quran often notice when similar phrases repeat across different prophet stories. Some might think, "Why give us the same thing again? Just replace one word and move on." But this repetition serves a powerful purpose. The Quraysh hearing these accounts over and over are being told: this happened, then it happened exactly the same way again, then it happened yet again. The pattern is undeniable. What makes you think it won't happen to you?

Even stylistically, the language embeds this reality into your consciousness. The sameness itself becomes the message. History repeats. Divine justice repeats. The destruction of wrongdoing nations repeats. Why would you be the exception?

Allah ﷻ describes Himself here with two magnificent names: Al-Qawiyy (the Ultimately Mighty) and Al-Aziz (the Final Authority). These names remind us that when Allah's ﷻ decision comes, no force can resist it, and no authority can override it.

translation: And the shriek seized those who had wronged, and they became within their homes [corpses] fallen prone
commentary:
وَأَخَذَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دِيَـٰرِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ

A loud scream seized those who did wrong, and by morning they lay lifeless in their homes, bodies collapsed and motionless.

The word الصَّيْحَةُ refers to a terrifying sound, a loud explosion or scream that overwhelmed them. The term جَـٰثِمِينَ describes bodies turned over, collapsed in death. This was their end—sudden, complete and irreversible.

translation: As if they had never prospered therein. Unquestionably, Thamud denied their Lord; then, away with Thamud
commentary:
كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَآ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ثَمُودَا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّثَمُودَ

As though they had never lived in luxury there. Indeed, Thamud was ungrateful to their Master. Away with Thamud!

The phrase "as though they had never lived in luxury" carries profound irony. Allah ﷻ is alluding to the fact that these homes were beautiful, their town was prosperous, their settlement something people would want to move to. Yet now, in destruction, it appears as if no moment of comfort or prosperity ever existed there.

Notice the construction كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ —"they disbelieved in their Master." The preposition ب is missing. In Arabic, when you say كَفَرَ with the preposition ب, it emphasizes disbelief. Without it, the word carries both meanings: ingratitude and disbelief. Thamud was ungrateful to their Master and they disbelieved in Him. Both crimes combined.

The ayah concludes with أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّثَمُودَ—"Away with Thamud!" This is a declaration of finality. Done. Finished. No return.

translation: And certainly did Our messengers come to Abraham with good tidings; they said, "Peace." He said, "Peace," and did not delay in bringing [them] a roasted calf
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ بِٱلْبُشْرَىٰ قَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا ۖ قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ ۖ فَمَا لَبِثَ أَن جَآءَ بِعِجْلٍ حَنِيذٍ

Our messengers came to Ibrahim (AS) with good news. They said, "Salaman" (peace). He replied, "Salamun" (peace). Without delay, he brought a roasted calf.

We've just heard about three pre-Abrahamic prophets—Nuh, Hud and Salih (peace be upon them all). Now Ibrahim (AS) appears in the central position of this surah, and after him we'll encounter post-Abrahamic narratives. Why is Ibrahim (AS) placed at this pivotal point? Because he's the central figure in Arab history, the one who ties all these nations together. The Christians trace themselves to him, the Jews claim descent from him, and the pagan Arabs of Makkah acknowledged their connection to him through Ismail (AS). He's the unifying ancestor, so he's given central position.

Interestingly, this isn't even Ibrahim's story in the traditional sense. It's really about the guests—the angels—and their mission. But Ibrahim (AS) is brought up because of his historical significance and his connection to what's about to unfold.

The angels entered and said سَلَـٰمًا—peace, in the accusative form, which is softer. Ibrahim (AS) responded with سَلَـٰمٌ—peace in the nominative, which carries more strength and warmth. His greeting is actually more emphatic and welcoming than theirs.

Then فَمَا لَبِثَ—he didn't delay at all. The root ل ب ث means to stay somewhere for a long time, to linger. The negation here means the opposite: he didn't chill, he didn't wait around. The moment guests arrived, he immediately thought, "How do I honor them?" He rushed to prepare a roasted calf for them. This is the hospitality of prophets.

translation: But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he distrusted them and felt from them apprehension. They said, "Fear not. We have been sent to the people of Lot
commentary:
فَلَمَّا رَءَآ أَيْدِيَهُمْ لَا تَصِلُ إِلَيْهِ نَكِرَهُمْ وَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً ۚ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِ لُوطٍ

When he saw their hands not reaching toward it, he became suspicious of them and felt a hidden fear. They said, "Don't be afraid. We've been sent to the people of Lut (AS)."

Ibrahim (AS) noticed something deeply unsettling: their hands weren't reaching for the food. To understand why this troubled him so much, we need historical context. In that era, assassins existed who would come to kill someone. These assassins had an honor code: if they ate from your food, they wouldn't harm you. Eating from someone meant they had shown you kindness, and that protected you.

So when Ibrahim (AS) saw them not even touching his food, alarm bells went off. The Quran uses three different words to describe his fear, each revealing something distinct.

First, نَكِرَهُمْ—"he became suspicious of them." He got a little weirded out. This can't be good.

Then أَوْجَسَ—he felt a hidden fear inside. This verb أَوْجَسَ specifically means to conceal your terror while maintaining composure outwardly. He's still smiling, still playing the host: "How's it going?" But internally, he's terrified.

The word خِيفَةً describes a fear that develops from noticing certain hints. Imagine you're in the woods and hear something—you look around, that anxious awareness is kheefah. It's a fear that comes from picking up on signals that something dangerous might be approaching.

But these guests were angels, and they knew what he was feeling despite his efforts to hide it. So they immediately reassured him: لَا تَخَفْ—"Don't be afraid." Then they revealed their true mission: إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِ لُوطٍ—"We've been sent to the nation of Lut (AS)." Yes, they're here to destroy a people, but not Ibrahim (AS). Not his household.

translation: And his Wife was standing, and she smiled. Then We gave her good tidings of Isaac and after Isaac, Jacob
commentary:
وَٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ قَآئِمَةٌ فَضَحِكَتْ فَبَشَّرْنَـٰهَا بِإِسْحَـٰقَ وَمِن وَرَآءِ إِسْحَـٰقَ يَعْقُوبَ

His wife was standing nearby, and she laughed. Then We gave her glad tidings of Ishaq (AS), and beyond Ishaq, Yaqub (AS).

This is Sarah, Ibrahim's (AS) second wife. Hajar (RA) has already been settled in Makkah. Ismail (AS) has already been born. All of that history has already unfolded. Now the younger son, Ishaq (AS), is about to be born to Sarah.

She was standing—قَآئِمَةٌ—and فَضَحِكَتْ—she laughed. But why? She hasn't been told about the baby yet. What's she laughing about? She's laughing because she's been afraid. These mysterious guests who wouldn't eat, the tension in the air—and now they've revealed they're going to destroy the people of Lut (AS). It's not about her family. She's relieved. "It's about time," she might have thought. "Finally, they're leaving."

But right at that moment of laughter, the angels give her news that changes everything: فَبَشَّرْنَـٰهَا بِإِسْحَـٰقَ—"We gave her glad tidings of Ishaq." Not just that—وَمِن وَرَآءِ إِسْحَـٰقَ يَعْقُوبَ—"and beyond Ishaq, Yaqub." She's being told she'll have a son, and that son will grow up to give her a grandson. Two generations of prophets announced in one breath.

The laughter disappears.

translation: She said, "Woe to me! Shall I give birth while I am an old woman and this, my husband, is an old man? Indeed, this is an amazing thing
commentary:
قَالَتْ يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ ءَأَلِدُ وَأَنَا۠ عَجُوزٌ وَهَـٰذَا بَعْلِى شَيْخًا ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ

She said, "Woe to me! Shall I give birth while I'm an old woman, and this—my husband—is an old man? This is truly an amazing thing!"

يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ—"Oh my goodness! What terrible thing is this?" She's in complete shock. Her response is deeply human and expressive. 

ءَأَلِدُ وَأَنَا۠ عَجُوزٌ—"Am I going to give birth when I'm this old?" She's well beyond childbearing years. Then she gestures: وَهَـٰذَا بَعْلِى شَيْخًا—"And this—my husband—look at him! He's an old man!" The way she says شَيْخًا has a tone to it. "Have you seen him? He's elderly!" There's an exclamation mark, a question mark, shock and disbelief all wrapped into her words.

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ—"This is really strange! This is bizarre!" She's processing the impossible. And notice—she's saying all this directly to angels. She's remarkably expressive, speaking her mind completely. This woman doesn't hold back.

translation: They said, "Are you amazed at the decree of Allah? May the mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, people of the house. Indeed, He is Praiseworthy and Honorable
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَعْجَبِينَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ رَحْمَتُ ٱللَّهِ وَبَرَكَـٰتُهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَمِيدٌ مَّجِيدٌ

They said, "Are you amazed by Allah's command? May the mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, people of the house. Indeed, He is Praiseworthy and Glorious."

The angels respond with gentle correction: أَتَعْجَبِينَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ—"Are you shocked by Allah's ﷻ command?" Why would you be amazed? This is Allah ﷻ we're talking about. Nothing is beyond His power.

Then they make a beautiful supplication: رَحْمَتُ ٱللَّهِ وَبَرَكَـٰتُهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ—"May Allah's ﷻ mercy and His blessings be upon you, people of the house." 

Notice the phrase أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ—the people of the house. This teaches us the Quranic definition of "household": husband and wife, and their future children. This is a family unit being blessed by angels.

These words—رَحْمَتُ ٱللَّهِ وَبَرَكَـٰتُهُۥ—are actually farewell words in Arabic culture. They're said when someone is about to leave. The angels are subtly indicating: "We've delivered our message. Congratulations. May your family be blessed. It's time for us to go."

They conclude with إِنَّهُۥ حَمِيدٌ مَّجِيدٌ—"He is Praiseworthy, Glorious." Allah ﷻ is حَمِيدٌ—praised in and of Himself, deserving of all praise. He is مَّجِيدٌ—full of majesty. Everything He does carries magnificence. Don't think this miracle is beneath Him or strange for Him. This is part of His glory. Embrace it.

translation: And when the fright had left Abraham and the good tidings had reached him, he began to argue with Us concerning the people of Lot
commentary:
فَلَمَّا ذَهَبَ عَنْ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلرَّوْعُ وَجَآءَتْهُ ٱلْبُشْرَىٰ يُجَـٰدِلُنَا فِى قَوْمِ لُوطٍ

When the shock left Ibrahim (AS) and the glad tidings reached him, he began debating with Us concerning the people of Lut (AS).

So much has happened in such a short time. Ibrahim (AS) has experienced multiple waves of fear and surprise. Let's map out what he's been processing: mysterious guests arrive, they won't eat his food (alarming), they reveal they're angels (relief), they announce the destruction of Lut's (AS) people (shock), his wife starts speaking boldly to angels (more shock), she's given news of a son (overwhelming), and a grandson too (even more overwhelming). And through all this, there's still an uneaten roasted calf sitting there.

Three different Arabic words have been used for Ibrahim's (AS) fear throughout this passage, and now we encounter a third: ٱلرَّوْعُ. This word describes nervous, overwhelming fear—the kind that makes you unable to think clearly. It comes from the word used for a she-camel that becomes so terrified by seeing a predator that she goes crazy, doesn't recognize her master and can't be calmed down. That's raw'. A state of shock and panic combined.

When that finally subsided—فَلَمَّا ذَهَبَ عَنْ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلرَّوْعُ—when he finally processed everything and calmed down, what did he do? The good news about his son helped settle his mind, but then something else gripped his attention: these angels had said they're going to completely annihilate Lut's (AS) nation.

So يُجَـٰدِلُنَا فِى قَوْمِ لُوطٍ—he began to debate with the angels concerning the people of Lut (AS). Some narrations tell us what this debate looked like. Ibrahim (AS) started asking: "What if there were fifty believers among them? Would you still destroy them?" The angels said no. "What if there were forty? Thirty? Twenty?" It came down to five. And eventually, Allah ﷻ told him through the angels: just leave it. It's done. There isn't even one righteous person left besides Lut (AS) and his daughters.

translation: Indeed, Abraham was forbearing, grieving and [frequently] returning [to Allah]
commentary:
إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَحَلِيمٌ أَوَّٰهٌ مُّنِيبٌ

Indeed, Ibrahim (AS) was forbearing, deeply compassionate and constantly turning back to Allah.

This ayah explains why Ibrahim (AS) interceded. Allah ﷻ describes him with three beautiful qualities.

حَلِيمٌ—forbearing, so considerate of others. This word captures someone with almost motherly qualities of patience and gentleness. He's the kind of fatherly figure who embodies deep compassion.

أَوَّٰهٌ—this is a remarkable word in Arabic. It comes from أَوَّه, which describes someone who becomes so emotional before Allah ﷻ that they can't help but cry out. The sound أَوَّه itself is the expression of deep feeling, like a sob that escapes when you're overwhelmed. Ibrahim (AS) was so softhearted that when he would turn to Allah ﷻ in prayer, sobs would come out. His tenderness before His Master is captured in this single word.

Whenever you read a supplication recorded in the Quran from Ibrahim (AS), you should imagine him in tears. Picture him truly sobbing as he asks Allah ﷻ. That's the reality of أَوَّٰهٌ.

مُّنِيبٌ—constantly returning to Allah ﷻ. He felt like he had been away from Allah ﷻ too long, so he kept coming back, again and again. This is the quality of always turning back in repentance, in remembrance, in connection.

These three qualities explain his advocacy for Lut's (AS) people. This wasn't even his nation, yet he still interceded for them. That's the depth of his mercy.

translation: [The angels said], "O Abraham, give up this [plea]. Indeed, the command of your Lord has come, and indeed, there will reach them a punishment that cannot be repelled
commentary:
يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ قَدْ جَآءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَإِنَّهُمْ ءَاتِيهِمْ عَذَابٌ غَيْرُ مَرْدُودٍ

"O Ibrahim, turn away from this. Indeed, the command of your Master has come, and there is coming to them a punishment that cannot be repelled."

Compare this moment to when Nuh (AS) was told about his son. Allah ﷻ said to Nuh (AS): لَا تَسْـَٔلْنِ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ—"Don't ask Me about what you have no knowledge of" (11:46). That was a harsh verdict, a sharp correction.

But here? يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَآ—"O Ibrahim, just leave it. Turn away from this." The tone is completely different. It's gentle, almost like speaking to a close friend. "Don't worry about it. Let it go."

Why the difference? Because Allah ﷻ called Ibrahim (AS) خَلِيلَ ٱللَّهِ—the intimate friend of Allah. So He speaks to him like a friend. There's tenderness in the address.

إِنَّهُۥ قَدْ جَآءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ—"The decision of your Master has already come." It's done. وَإِنَّهُمْ ءَاتِيهِمْ عَذَابٌ غَيْرُ مَرْدُودٍ—"And there's coming to them a punishment that cannot be turned back, that won't be canceled or repelled."

The matter is settled. But even in finality, there's gentleness in how Allah ﷻ addresses His friend.

translation: And when Our messengers, [the angels], came to Lot, he was anguished for them and felt for them great discomfort and said, "This is a trying day
commentary:
وَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِىٓءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا وَقَالَ هَـٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَصِيبٌ

When Our messengers came to Lut (AS), he was distressed by their arrival, and his chest tightened because of them. He said, "This is a difficult day."

The same angels who visited Ibrahim (AS) now arrive at Lut's (AS) home. But notice the completely different reaction.

سِىٓءَ بِهِمْ—he was deeply disturbed by their presence. Some scholars understood this to mean the angels had already told him they'd come to destroy his nation, which caused his distress. But the context suggests otherwise. He didn't yet know they were angels. He just saw them: young, exceptionally handsome men. And he immediately knew the problem his nation had.

وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا—literally, "his arm became tight because of them." The word ذَرْعًا means arm, but it also represents one's capacity, one's power. His ability to protect them became constricted. In other words, he felt powerless. "I can't protect these guests. I don't have the strength to defend them against what's coming."

Now they're in his home—him and his daughters. There's no way he can shield them from his people. And we learn from other parts of the Quran that his wife wasn't on his side. In fact, she leaked the news: "There are a couple of men from out of town here." Word spread quickly among a people known for their perversion.

Nothing has happened yet. But Lut (AS) knows what's coming. So he says: هَـٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَصِيبٌ—"This is going to be a difficult day."

The word عَصِيبٌ comes from عَصَبَ, meaning tough, harsh, void of any goodness. "This day is going to be terrible. Oh boy." He's bracing himself for what he knows is about to unfold.

Reflect on the contrast: when these same guests came to Ibrahim (AS), what did he say? سَلَـٰمٌ—peace. He brought food, offered hospitality, engaged warmly. But when they arrive at Lut's (AS) home, his immediate reaction is dread. "Did you really have to come? This is going to be bad. This is just going to be bad."

We learn something profound here: the arrival of angels doesn't mean one thing. It means different things in different contexts. The same angels, on the same journey, made two stops with two very different missions.

translation: And his people came hastening to him, and before [this] they had been doing evil deeds. He said, "O my people, these are my daughters; they are purer for you. So fear Allah and do not disgrace me concerning my guests. Is there not among you a man of reason
commentary:
وَجَآءَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ يُهْرَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ وَمِن قَبْلُ كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ بَنَاتِى هُنَّ أَطْهَرُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُخْزُونِ فِى ضَيْفِىٓ ۖ أَلَيْسَ مِنكُمْ رَجُلٌ رَّشِيدٌ

The people of Lut (AS) came racing toward him, overwhelmed with emotion and passion. The phrase يُهْرَعُونَ captures a disturbing scene—they were running, screaming, and howling like a mob possessed, rushing toward his house as though driven by forces beyond their control. The passive form indicates they weren't even acting of their own accord, as if the devil himself had possessed them.

Allah ﷻ reminds us that وَمِن قَبْلُ كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ—even much before this incident, they were engaged in these filthy sins. This wasn't a momentary lapse but a deeply entrenched pattern of evil behavior.

Lut (AS) desperately tried to protect his guests, saying, "My nation, these are my daughters—they are more pure for you." There are two interpretations of this statement. Some scholars understand that as the prophet of his nation, he was like a father to them all, and thus he referred to the women of the community as "my daughters," urging them toward lawful marriage. Others suggest he literally offered his own daughters in marriage to divert them from their crime, though this interpretation is less convincing.

He pleaded with them: فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ—"Have consciousness of Allah!" Then he begged, وَلَا تُخْزُونِ فِى ضَيْفِىٓ—"Don't humiliate me regarding my guests." He concluded with a piercing question: أَلَيْسَ مِنكُمْ رَجُلٌ رَّشِيدٌ—"Is there not among you a single upright man?" The word رَّشِيد carries the meaning of someone who is straight, sensible, and morally sound. He was asking if there wasn't even one person of conscience left among them.

translation: They said, "You have already known that we have not concerning your daughters any claim, and indeed, you know what we want
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا لَنَا فِى بَنَاتِكَ مِنْ حَقٍّ وَإِنَّكَ لَتَعْلَمُ مَا نُرِيدُ

Their response was chilling in its shamelessness. They said, لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ—"You already know we have no right to your daughters." They weren't even pretending to be interested in lawful relationships with women. وَإِنَّكَ لَتَعْلَمُ مَا نُرِيدُ—"And you know exactly what we want." 

This statement reveals the complete moral bankruptcy of a people who had normalized their perversion to such a degree that they could openly declare their evil intentions without shame. They didn't hide behind excuses or justifications—they brazenly acknowledged their corrupt desires.

translation: He said, "If only I had against you some power or could take refuge in a strong support
commentary:
قَالَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِى بِكُمْ قُوَّةً أَوْ ءَاوِىٓ إِلَىٰ رُكْنٍ شَدِيدٍ

Lut (AS) expressed his utter frustration and helplessness: "If only I had strength against you, or could take refuge in a strong support." The word رُكْنٍ means a pillar or the strongest part of something, like a fortress that cannot be breached. He wished he had some powerful tribe or fortified place where he could protect himself and his guests from this mob.

This is the lament of an old prophet, completely overwhelmed by the wickedness of his people. Notice how the word آوِي—meaning to take refuge or run back—echoes a word used earlier in the surah. This repetition is part of the literary texture of Surah Hud, where words and themes return like echoes, drawing connections between different prophetic stories.

translation: The angels said, "O Lot, indeed we are messengers of your Lord; [therefore], they will never reach you.
