So set out with your family during a portion of the night and let not any among you look back - except your wife; indeed, she will be struck by that which strikes them. Indeed, their appointment is [for] the morning. Is not the morning near
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰلُوطُ إِنَّا رُسُلُ رَبِّكَ لَن يَصِلُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكَ ۖ فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَلَا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنكُمْ أَحَدٌ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَكَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مُصِيبُهَا مَآ أَصَابَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ مَوْعِدَهُمُ ٱلصُّبْحُ ۚ أَلَيْسَ ٱلصُّبْحُ بِقَرِيبٍ

At this critical moment, the angels who had remained silent throughout this ordeal finally revealed themselves. They said, "O Lut, we are messengers of your Master." What relief these words must have brought! لَن يَصِلُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكَ—"They will never reach you. They cannot harm you."

Then came the urgent command: فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ—"Travel with your family in the night." The word أَسْرِ itself means to travel at night, and the addition of بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ—"in a portion of the night"—adds urgency. They didn't have all night. They had only a limited window to escape.

The instruction continued: وَلَا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنكُمْ أَحَدٌ—"And let not any one of you look back." Don't turn around. Don't glance at what's happening. The horror that was about to unfold would be so traumatic that witnessing it would scar them for life.

Then came the painful exception: إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَكَ—"Except your wife." She would not be spared. إِنَّهُۥ مُصِيبُهَا مَآ أَصَابَهُمْ—"Indeed, what strikes them will strike her too." The word أَصَابَ means to hit a target precisely, like an arrow finding its mark. Keep this word in mind, for it will return with devastating effect.

The angels concluded: إِنَّ مَوْعِدَهُمُ ٱلصُّبْحُ—"Their appointed time is the morning." Then they added urgently, أَلَيْسَ ٱلصُّبْحُ بِقَرِيبٍ—"Isn't the morning already near?" This rhetorical question wasn't philosophical—it meant "Let's go! Morning is coming! Move!"

translation: So when Our command came, We made the highest part [of the city] its lowest and rained upon them stones of layered hard clay, [which were]
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا جَعَلْنَا عَـٰلِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ مَّنضُودٍ

When Allah's ﷻ command came, the punishment was catastrophic. جَعَلْنَا عَـٰلِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا—"We made its heights into its depths." The city was literally flipped upside down. Their skyscrapers became dungeons. The entire town of Petra and the surrounding cities were inverted in a geological catastrophe beyond comprehension.

But that wasn't all. وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ—"And We rained upon them stones of baked clay." The word سِجِّيل refers to hardened, burnt clay that becomes like small pebbles.

Then Allah ﷻ adds another detail: مَّنضُودٍ. This word comes from the same root as the word for teeth being lined up in a row. It means these stones didn't fall randomly—they came in volleys, in rows, in sequences. When one struck, ten or twelve more followed in rapid succession, all targeting the same person. They were being hit not once but repeatedly, with precision strikes one after another.

translation: Marked from your Lord. And Allah 's punishment is not from the wrongdoers [very] far
commentary:
مُّسَوَّمَةً عِندَ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا هِىَ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بِبَعِيدٍ

These stones were مُّسَوَّمَةً—branded, marked, labeled. Each stone carried the name of its victim, marked by your Master. Like modern warfare where missiles are painted with the names of target cities as mockery, or like ancient warriors who would write their victim's name on an arrow before shooting, these tiny pebbles had the names of their targets inscribed by divine decree. Each stone had "your name on it."

Then comes a chilling warning: وَمَا هِىَ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بِبَعِيدٍ—"And it is not far from the wrongdoers." This focused, targeted, sniper-fire punishment is not distant from any wrongdoers, whether in Lut's (AS) time or in ours.

translation: And to Madyan [We sent] their brother Shu'ayb. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. And do not decrease from the measure and the scale. Indeed, I see you in prosperity, but indeed, I fear for you the punishment of an all-encompassing Day
commentary:
وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ وَلَا تَنقُصُوا۟ ٱلْمِكْيَالَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ ۚ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُم بِخَيْرٍ وَإِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ مُّحِيطٍ

Now the narrative shifts to the people of Madyan and their prophet Shuʿayb (AS). Like every messenger, he began with the foundational call: "My people, worship Allah alone. You have no deity besides Him."

But then Shuʿayb (AS) addressed something specific to his people's corruption: وَلَا تَنقُصُوا۟ ٱلْمِكْيَالَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ—"And do not decrease in the measure and the scale." They were cheating people in business transactions, shortchanging them in weights and measures. This was their particular sin alongside their idolatry.

He added encouragingly, إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُم بِخَيْرٍ—"I see you in prosperity. I see good for you." But then came the warning: وَإِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ مُّحِيطٍ—"And I fear for you the punishment of an encompassing Day"—a day whose punishment will surround you completely, leaving no escape.

translation: And O my people, give full measure and weight in justice and do not deprive the people of their due and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption
commentary:
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ أَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْمِكْيَالَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ

Shuʿayb (AS) moved from prohibition to positive command: أَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْمِكْيَالَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ—"Give full measure and weight in justice." Don't just avoid cheating—actively fulfill your obligations with fairness.

وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ—"And do not deprive people of their due." Don't shortchange them. Don't give them less than what they deserve in their transactions.

وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ—"And do not go about the earth spreading corruption." This connects business ethics to broader social corruption. When cheating becomes normalized in commerce, it spreads like a disease through society. One merchant cheats and sells inferior products at lower prices, forcing competitors to become corrupt in their practices just to survive. Customers respond by paying with counterfeit money. Corruption breeds more corruption until it becomes the accepted norm.

Today's parallels are clear: unfair labor wages, child labor factories in developing countries making luxury brands sold in Western stores, pharmaceutical companies charging a thousand times the production cost for life-saving medicines while people die because they cannot afford treatment. These are modern manifestations of the same principle—people not being given their due measure and weight.

translation: What remains [lawful] from Allah is best for you, if you would be believers. But I am not a guardian over you
commentary:
بَقِيَّتُ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِحَفِيظٍ

Then Shuʿayb (AS) presented them with a powerful concept: بَقِيَّتُ ٱللَّهِ—"What remains from Allah" or "Allah's remainder." When you deal fairly and honestly, you won't make as much profit as when you cheat. You'll miss out on certain percentages. Whatever is left over after your honest dealing—consider that the remainder Allah ﷻ wants for you.

Notice he didn't even call it profit (ribh) or provision (rizq). He called it what's "left over"—almost nothing compared to what you were making through dishonest means. But خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ—it is far better for you إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ—if you are truly believers.

He concluded: وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِحَفِيظٍ—"And I am not a guardian over you." He wasn't there to police them or force compliance. His role was to advise and warn, not to coerce.

translation: They said, "O Shu'ayb, does your prayer command you that we should leave what our fathers worship or not do with our wealth what we please? Indeed, you are the forbearing, the discerning
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ أَصَلَوٰتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ أَن نَّتْرُكَ مَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَآ أَوْ أَن نَّفْعَلَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِنَا مَا نَشَـٰٓؤُا۟ ۖ إِنَّكَ لَأَنتَ ٱلْحَلِيمُ ٱلرَّشِيدُ

Their response reveals a timeless strategy of those who reject divine guidance. They said mockingly, أَصَلَوٰتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ—"Is it your prayer that commands you?" In other words: "Keep praying all you want, but why are you bringing religion into our business practices? Why does your prayer make you talk about what our ancestors worshipped?"

This is ancient secularism—the separation of religion from public life. Prayer is fine as a personal matter, they argued, but it has nothing to do with the marketplace, with economics, with how we run our businesses. أَن نَّفْعَلَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِنَا مَا نَشَـٰٓؤُا۟—"Or that we do with our wealth as we please?" Why should your religion tell us about our fiscal responsibilities?

Then came the sarcastic conclusion: إِنَّكَ لَأَنتَ ٱلْحَلِيمُ ٱلرَّشِيدُ—"Indeed, you are the forbearing, the rightly guided one!" This was pure mockery. "Look at you, so righteous, so forbearing. You think because you pray, you're better than us and can tell us how to live?"

translation: He said, "O my people, have you considered: if I am upon clear evidence from my Lord and He has provided me with a good provision from Him...? And I do not intend to differ from you in that which I have forbidden you; I only intend reform as much as I am able. And my success is not but through Allah. Upon him I have relied, and to Him I return
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَرَزَقَنِى مِنْهُ رِزْقًا حَسَنًا ۚ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخَالِفَكُمْ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنْهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ ۚ إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا ٱلْإِصْلَـٰحَ مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُ ۚ وَمَا تَوْفِيقِىٓ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ

Shuʿayb (AS) responded with dignified patience. أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى—"Have you considered: what if I stand on clear evidence from my Master?"

Notice the subtle difference in how he described Allah's ﷻ blessing: وَرَزَقَنِى مِنْهُ رِزْقًا حَسَنًا—"And He has provided me good provision from Him." Other prophets mentioned being given special mercy, but Shuʿayb (AS) highlighted provision—directly relevant to his people's obsession with corrupt profits. Allah ﷻ had given him good, lawful provision, proving that honesty doesn't lead to poverty.

وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخَالِفَكُمْ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنْهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ—"And I do not want to oppose you by doing what I forbid you from." In other words, "I'm not a hypocrite. I don't forbid you from something and then do it myself. I get no joy from opposing you."

إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا ٱلْإِصْلَـٰحَ—"I only want reform"—the exact opposite of the corruption they were spreading. مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُ—"As much as I am able." He acknowledged his human limitations.

Then came a profound declaration of dependence: وَمَا تَوْفِيقِىٓ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ—"And my success is not but through Allah." His ability to accomplish anything, his competency, his success—all of it depended entirely on Allah ﷻ. عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ—"Upon Him I have relied," وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ—"and to Him I return in repentance."

translation: And O my people, let not [your] dissension from me cause you to be struck by that similar to what struck the people of Noah or the people of Hud or the people of Salih. And the people of Lot are not from you far away
commentary:
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ لَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شِقَاقِىٓ أَن يُصِيبَكُم مِّثْلُ مَآ أَصَابَ قَوْمَ نُوحٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ هُودٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ صَـٰلِحٍ ۚ وَمَا قَوْمُ لُوطٍ مِّنكُم بِبَعِيدٍ

Shuʿayb (AS) warned them urgently: لَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شِقَاقِىٓ—"Let not your opposition to me cause" أَن يُصِيبَكُم—"that there strike you مِّثْلُ مَآ أَصَابَ—"the likes of what struck" previous nations.

He listed them: the people of Nuh (AS), the people of Hud (AS), the people of Salih (AS). These were distant histories that this surah had already recounted.

But then he added: وَمَا قَوْمُ لُوطٍ مِّنكُم بِبَعِيدٍ—"And the people of Lut are not far from you." This was recent history, contemporary events. The word أَصَابَ—"to strike precisely"—echoes back to the stones that struck Lut's (AS) people. That targeted, precise punishment isn't far-fetched. It's not ancient history. It just happened, and it's not distant from you either.

translation: And ask forgiveness of your Lord and then repent to Him. Indeed, my Lord is Merciful and Affectionate
commentary:
وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ

Despite their mockery and rejection, Shuʿayb (AS) still called them to repentance. وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ—"Seek forgiveness from your Master," ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ—"then turn back to Him in repentance."

He reminded them of Allah's ﷻ beautiful names: إِنَّ رَبِّى رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ—"Indeed, my Master is Most Merciful, Most Loving." The name الودود is particularly tender—it speaks of deep, affectionate love. Even after all their corruption and mockery, the door to Allah's ﷻ mercy and love remained open.

translation: They said, "O Shu'ayb, we do not understand much of what you say, and indeed, we consider you among us as weak. And if not for your family, we would have stoned you [to death]; and you are not to us one respected
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ مَا نَفْقَهُ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا تَقُولُ وَإِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِينَا ضَعِيفًا ۖ وَلَوْلَا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْنَـٰكَ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْنَا بِعَزِيزٍ

Their response demonstrates a classic tactic of undermining someone's message. مَا نَفْقَهُ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا تَقُولُ—"We don't understand much of what you say." This is an easy way to deflect blame and make the speaker feel inadequate. Shuʿayb (AS) spoke clearly and eloquently, but by claiming they couldn't understand him, they made it seem like the fault was his. "I just don't get what you're talking about. It doesn't make any sense."

Then they attacked his status: وَإِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِينَا ضَعِيفًا—"And indeed, we see you among us as weak." Even if we understood you, you're powerless. What can you do?

The new word here is رَهْطُ, which has several meanings. It can mean a group, tribal leadership, or specifically the young men of a tribe on whom all hopes are placed—the elite warriors and future leaders. وَلَوْلَا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْنَـٰكَ—"And if not for your tribe, we would have stoned you." The only thing protecting you is your citizenship, your connection to important people. But that protection is temporary.

وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْنَا بِعَزِيزٍ—"And you are not dear to us" or "you are not difficult for us to kill." You have no authority over us, and your life means nothing to us.

translation: He said, "O my people, is my family more respected for power by you than Allah? But you put Him behind your backs [in neglect]. Indeed, my Lord is encompassing of what you do
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَهْطِىٓ أَعَزُّ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَٱتَّخَذْتُمُوهُ وَرَآءَكُمْ ظِهْرِيًّا ۖ إِنَّ رَبِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ

Shuʿayb (AS) exposed the absurdity of their position with a devastating question: أَرَهْطِىٓ أَعَزُّ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ—"Is my tribe mightier to you than Allah?" You respect my tribal connections enough to hold back from killing me, but you have no respect for Allah ﷻ?

وَٱتَّخَذْتُمُوهُ وَرَآءَكُمْ ظِهْرِيًّا—"And you have taken Him behind your backs." The word ظِهْرِيًّا comes from the root for "back" (dhahr). You've completely turned your backs on Allah ﷻ, casting Him far behind you, treating Him as irrelevant while you fear my tribe more than you fear Him.

إِنَّ رَبِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ—"Indeed, my Master is encompassing of what you do." He's not distant or unaware. He surrounds all your actions and will hold you accountable.

translation: And O my people, work according to your position; indeed, I am working. You are going to know to whom will come a punishment that will disgrace him and who is a liar. So watch; indeed, I am with you a watcher, [awaiting the outcome]
commentary:
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنِّى عَـٰمِلٌ ۖ سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَمَنْ هُوَ كَـٰذِبٌ ۖ وَٱرْتَقِبُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ رَقِيبٌ

Finally, Shuʿayb (AS) issued a challenge. ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ—"Do whatever you're going to do. Hold your ground. Stay in your position." إِنِّى عَـٰمِلٌ—"I'm doing what I'm going to do."

سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ—"You will soon know" مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ—"who will be struck by a punishment that humiliates him" وَمَنْ هُوَ كَـٰذِبٌ—"and who is a liar."

وَٱرْتَقِبُوٓا۟—"And watch carefully." This word مُرْتَقِب is more intense than ordinary waiting (muntadhir). It means to watch cautiously, to wait in anticipation of an enemy's attack, to be on guard.

إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ رَقِيبٌ—"Indeed, I am with you watching." A رَقِيب is someone who watches over something with power and authority. They had just called him weak, but he declared himself a watcher with authority—because his authority came from Allah ﷻ.

translation: And when Our command came, We saved Shu'ayb and those who believed with him, by mercy from Us. And the shriek seized those who had wronged, and they became within their homes [corpses] fallen prone
commentary:
وَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا شُعَيْبًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَأَخَذَتِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دِيَـٰرِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ

When Allah's ﷻ decree finally arrived, وَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا—"When Our command came," نَجَّيْنَا شُعَيْبًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ—"We saved Shuʿayb and those who believed with him" بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا—"by a mercy from Us."

وَأَخَذَتِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ—"And the loud cry seized those who wronged." A صَيْحَة  can be a piercing scream, an explosive sound, or an overwhelming blast. It grabbed them completely.

فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دِيَـٰرِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ—"And by morning they were in their homes, fallen prostrate"—corpses lying twisted and mangled, toppled over where they fell.

translation: As if they had never prospered therein. Then, away with Madyan as Thamud was taken away
commentary:
كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَآ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّمَدْيَنَ كَمَا بَعِدَتْ ثَمُودُ

كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَآ—"As though they had never prospered therein." The word يَغْنَوْا suggests living in luxury, enjoying prosperity and comfort. It was as if all their years of comfortable living, all their wealth accumulated through corruption, had never existed.

أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّمَدْيَنَ—"Away with Madyan!" or "Destruction to Madyan!" كَمَا بَعِدَتْ ثَمُودُ—"Just as Thamud was cast away." They joined the destroyed nations that came before them.

translation: And We did certainly send Moses with Our signs and a clear authority
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ

The surah now transitions to the story of Musa (AS), though only briefly mentioning its beginning. وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ—"And We certainly sent Musa" بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا—"with Our signs"—miraculous proofs of his prophethood.

وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ—"And a clear authority." This wasn't just spiritual guidance; Musa (AS) came with manifest, undeniable authority that could not be dismissed or ignored.

translation: To Pharaoh and his establishment, but they followed the command of Pharaoh, and the command of Pharaoh was not [at all] discerning
commentary:
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۖ وَمَآ أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ

إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ—"To Pharaoh and his chiefs." The message was directed at the leadership, the elite of Egyptian society.

فَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ—"But they followed the command of Pharaoh." Notice: they didn't follow the clear signs and authority that Musa (AS) brought. Instead, the chiefs and nobles followed Pharaoh's orders.

وَمَآ أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ—"And the command of Pharaoh was not rightly guided." The word رَشِيد—"rightly guided" or "straight"—echoes back through the surah. Lut (AS) asked if there was a single rasheed man among his people. The people of Madyan mockingly called Shuʿayb (AS) rasheed. Now we're told Pharaoh's command was decidedly not rasheed. His leadership, his decision-making, his entire system was crooked, misguided, and leading to destruction.

This establishes the pattern that will continue: misguided leadership leads entire nations to ruin, while the messengers of Allah ﷻ call to the straight path—even when they're mocked, threatened, and rejected.

translation: He will precede his people on the Day of Resurrection and lead them into the Fire; and wretched is the place to which they are led
commentary:
يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْوِرْدُ ٱلْمَوْرُودُ

The last thing Pharaoh did in this worldly life was lead his people in a particular direction, taking his chieftains with him until they drowned. Now Allah ﷻ shows us what his next steps will be—but these steps will occur on the Day of Judgment. The ayah states: يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (He will be taking steps and being ahead of his nation on the Day of Resurrection).

The verb used here is powerful in its irony. The word أَوْرَدَهُمُ means to lead someone to water. A وِرْد is a place where water is found, a reservoir. The last thing Pharaoh did in his earthly existence was lead his people to water—the sea—where they met their destruction. Now Allah ﷻ continues this imagery on Judgment Day, making him perform Iraq again, leading them to water once more. Except this time, فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ (he will lead them to the reservoir of fire instead).

The ayah concludes: وَبِئْسَ ٱلْوِرْدُ ٱلْمَوْرُودُ (What a horrible drink, what a horrible reservoir). The word الْوِرْدُ refers to the thirsty one seeking water, and here the primary thirsty one is Pharaoh himself. الْمَوْرُودُ refers both to the one who has been followed by others seeking water and to the drink itself that was found. What a wretched drinker, and what a horrific drink awaits them.

translation: And they were followed in this [world] with a curse and on the Day of Resurrection. And wretched is the gift which is given
commentary:
وَأُتْبِعُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِۦ لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ بِئْسَ ٱلرِّفْدُ ٱلْمَرْفُودُ

They were followed in this world with a curse, and on the Day of Resurrection as well. The word رِفْد is used when you help someone with charity—when someone is completely helpless and you offer them assistance, however small. It's an expression of complete dependency.

Consider the profound humiliation: Pharaoh, who was so full of arrogance in this world, will be utterly helpless on Judgment Day. The fire being poured down his throat, Allah ﷻ calls it a "charity" to these helpless people. بِئْسَ ٱلرِّفْدُ ٱلْمَرْفُودُ (What a horrible charity that is being delivered).

translation: That is from the news of the cities, which We relate to you; of them, some are [still] standing and some are [as] a harvest [mowed down]
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ نَقُصُّهُۥ عَلَيْكَ ۖ مِنْهَا قَآئِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ

That is from the news of the multiple towns that We are narrating to you. These narratives of destroyed nations are not merely about ancient struggles—they reflect the present struggle of the Messenger ﷺ. I am not only speaking about Pharaoh and his chiefs being sent to Hell; I am speaking about the chiefs of Makkah who oppose you. The parallels are constant throughout this surah.

The people of Salih (AS) said, "If it wasn't for your tribe, we would have killed you." The people of Shu'ayb (AS) said, "If it wasn't for your status, we would have killed you." These are the same threats the Messenger ﷺ faced. مِنْهَا قَآئِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ (Among them are those that are still standing, and those that have been completely harvested). 

قَآئِمٌ implies not only that remnants of these nations still exist, but that physical remains stand—the ruins of 'Ad and Thamud, the pyramids of Pharaoh's Egypt. حَصِيدٌ describes what has been completely plucked from the earth, like a farm scraped clean after harvest, leaving only stumps. That's what remains of some nations.

translation: And We did not wrong them, but they wronged themselves. And they were not availed at all by their gods which they invoked other than Allah when there came the command of your Lord. And they did not increase them in other than ruin
commentary:
وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۖ فَمَآ أَغْنَتْ عَنْهُمْ ءَالِهَتُهُمُ ٱلَّتِى يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ لَّمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا زَادُوهُمْ غَيْرَ تَتْبِيبٍ

We did not wrong them—they wronged themselves. Their false gods, which they called upon besides Allah ﷻ, benefited them in no way whatsoever when the decision of your Master arrived. The word أَغْنَتْ means to make someone free of need, to enrich them or provide benefit.

The ayah introduces a new word: تَتْبِيبٍ. The root is دَبَّ, which becomes تَتْبِيب in this intensified form—destruction upon destruction, curse upon curse. Their false gods didn't just fail to help them; they increased their ruin exponentially.

translation: And thus is the seizure of your Lord when He seizes the cities while they are committing wrong. Indeed, His seizure is painful and severe
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذَآ أَخَذَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَهِىَ ظَـٰلِمَةٌ ۚ إِنَّ أَخْذَهُۥٓ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ

And that is how the seizure of your Master is when He seizes a town while it is engaged in wrongdoing. إِنَّ أَخْذَهُۥٓ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ (His seizure is painful and intense). When Allah ﷻ takes hold of an oppressive society, His grasp is both devastating and inescapable.

translation: Indeed in that is a sign for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter. That is a Day for which the people will be collected, and that is a Day [which will be] witnessed
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّمَنْ خَافَ عَذَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّجْمُوعٌ لَّهُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّشْهُودٌ

In all of this there is a powerful sign for the one who fears the punishment of the Hereafter. That is a day for which people are going to be gathered, and that is a day that will be witnessed. The construction here is important: يَوْمٌ مَّجْمُوعٌ لَّهُ ٱلنَّاسُ is a complete sentence serving as an adjective of "that day"—a day for which all people will be gathered. There will not be a single human being who won't witness it.

translation: And We do not delay it except for a limited term
commentary:
وَمَا نُؤَخِّرُهُۥٓ إِلَّا لِأَجَلٍ مَّعْدُودٍ

We are not delaying it except for a very limited, counted deadline. The word مَّعْدُودٍ emphasizes that this is not an indefinite postponement—it is precisely measured and approaching.

translation: The Day it comes no soul will speak except by His permission. And among them will be the wretched and the prosperous
commentary:
يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لَا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِىٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ

The day it comes, no soul will speak even a little bit except by His permission. The verb here is لَا تَكَلَّمُ, not لَا تَتَكَلَّمُ—they won't speak in the least. فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِىٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ (Among them will be the wretched and the blessed)—those engaged in wretchedness and those who found deep, genuine happiness emanating from within.

translation: As for those who were [destined to be] wretched, they will be in the Fire. For them therein is [violent] exhaling and inhaling
commentary:
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ شَقُوا۟ فَفِى ٱلنَّارِ لَهُمْ فِيهَا زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ

As for those who were wretched, they will be in the Fire. We just learned they won't be able to talk, so what sound will they hear? لَهُمْ فِيهَا زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ (For them therein will be exhaling and inhaling).

زَفِير is used for a donkey's loud exhale when exhausted, and over time it grows louder as the panting worsens. شَهِيق is used for its inhaling—once you remove the load, the panting slowly calms down. What we're learning is that Hellfire itself is a creature with a growl as it inhales and exhales. That will be the only sound they experience.

translation: [They will be] abiding therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord should will. Indeed, your Lord is an effecter of what He intends
commentary:
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِّمَا يُرِيدُ

They will remain in it as long as the heavens and earth remain, except for what your Master wills. Because of this ayah, significant theological debate emerged in Islamic scholarly history. Great scholars of theology debated the implications when Allah ﷻ says people will remain in Hellfire "as long as the heavens and earth remain" instead of simply saying "forever."

Two historical figures who were otherwise at complete odds—Ibn 'Arabi and Ibn Taymiyyah—held the same minority opinion on this matter: that Hellfire may not actually be eternal but rather a very, very long time. This position was based on textual hints, including this phrase مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ (as long as the heavens and earth remain) and the exception إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ (except what your Master wills), which leaves the matter open rather than definitively closed.

The vast majority of scholars maintain that Hellfire is eternal. This scholarly debate should not distract us from the practical reality: whether Hellfire is forever or an unimaginably long time, Allah ﷻ guarantees us it is horrific even for a short duration. Getting caught up in these debates becomes counterproductive to our purpose on earth. Solving this mystery won't help us on Judgment Day—our focus should be on avoiding Hellfire altogether.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِّمَا يُرِيدُ (No doubt, your Master repeatedly does whatever He wants).

translation: And as for those who were [destined to be] prosperous, they will be in Paradise, abiding therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord should will - a bestowal uninterrupted
commentary:
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا۟ فَفِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ ۖ عَطَآءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ

As for those who have been brought joy, they will be in Paradise, remaining in it as long as the heavens and earth remain, except for what your Master wills. Then a beautiful addition: عَطَآءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ (a gift that will never be cut off).

The new word here is مَجْذُوذ from the root جَذَّ, meaning to cut something, to break something into pieces, to shatter something fragile. This gift will never be broken up, never be taken apart, never diminished. At least Paradise is permanent—let's focus on getting there.

translation: So do not be in doubt, [O Muhammad], as to what these [polytheists] are worshipping. They worship not except as their fathers worshipped before. And indeed, We will give them their share undiminished
commentary:
فَلَا تَكُ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّمَّا يَعْبُدُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ۚ مَا يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا كَمَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُهُم مِّن قَبْلُ ۚ وَإِنَّا لَمُوَفُّوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ غَيْرَ مَنقُوصٍ

Then you shouldn't be in any doubt regarding what these people worship. They're not worshiping anything different from what their forefathers used to worship long before them. They're simply imitating ancestral practices without reflection. وَإِنَّا لَمُوَفُّوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ غَيْرَ مَنقُوصٍ (We will give them their full portion without any reduction). Whatever consequences their worship deserves, they will receive in full.

translation: And We had certainly given Moses the Scripture, but it came under disagreement. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been judged between them. And indeed they are, concerning the Qur'an, in disquieting doubt
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَٱخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ

We had already given Musa (AS) the Book, and differences were created within it. Had a word and verdict not come from your Master beforehand, the matter would have been judged between them immediately. وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ (They are in a doubt about it that is continually growing).

This refers to doubt about Judgment Day. There is no religion I know of that has more confusion about the Day of Judgment than even orthodox Jewish tradition—the concept has become deeply mixed up over time. This serves as consolation to the Messenger ﷺ: even Musa (AS) faced a people who doubted and differed regarding divine revelation.

translation: And indeed, each [of the believers and disbelievers] - your Lord will fully compensate them for their deeds. Indeed, He is Acquainted with what they do
commentary:
وَإِنَّ كُلًّا لَّمَّا لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ رَبُّكَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ

No doubt about it—everyone, when Allah ﷻ gives them their deeds in full... there will come a time when Allah ﷻ will give everyone their deeds completely. When that happens, you will realize: إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (He has full awareness of what you are doing).

translation: So remain on a right course as you have been commanded, [you] and those who have turned back with you [to Allah], and do not transgress. Indeed, He is Seeing of what you do
commentary:
فَٱسْتَقِمْ كَمَآ أُمِرْتَ وَمَن تَابَ مَعَكَ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

So remain firm as you have been commanded, and whoever has repented and stands with you. وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ (And don't rebel). What would constitute rebellion in this context? For the Companions (RA), rebellion would mean not staying firm on their mission.

For the Sahaba (RA), Islam was different than it is for us. Islam meant staying by the Messenger's ﷺ side and continuing to call to this mission no matter what happens. This is what it meant to be Muslim in Makkah. The details of halal and haram, clothing restrictions, dietary laws—all of that came later. Right then, being Muslim meant: you stay with the Messenger ﷺ and continue calling with him, and whatever he tells you to do, you do, regardless of how much trouble it brings from your society and family. If you don't do this, you're rebelling.

إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (He is in full view of what you're doing). Notice how advice to the Muslims and warnings to the disbelievers are woven together in this conclusion. Allah ﷻ sometimes addresses the Muslims, then the Prophet ﷺ, then back to the Muslims. This is the concluding message—what you should take away from all the prophetic narratives you've heard.

translation: And do not incline toward those who do wrong, lest you be touched by the Fire, and you would not have other than Allah any protectors; then you would not be helped
commentary:
وَلَا تَرْكَنُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ فَتَمَسَّكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ

Don't lean toward those who have done wrong. رَكَنَ in Arabic means to lean on something stronger than yourself to recuperate. Perhaps the thinking was: "We should have good relations with the leaders of Quraysh because they have so much power. They'll give us breathing room, and then we can come back and call to Islam again later."

Allah ﷻ says: Don't do that. Don't lean on them at all. فَتَمَسَّكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ (The Fire will touch you). وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ (You won't find any protective friends besides Allah ﷻ). Don't seek friendship or alliance anywhere else. ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ (Then you will not be aided).

translation: And establish prayer at the two ends of the day and at the approach of the night. Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds. That is a reminder for those who remember
commentary:
وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ طَرَفَىِ ٱلنَّهَارِ وَزُلَفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْحَسَنَـٰتِ يُذْهِبْنَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ذِكْرَىٰ لِلذَّٰكِرِينَ

You want help? You want to lean on something? Lean on what? Salah. Seek aid not from enemies but from patience and prayer. Establish the prayer طَرَفَىِ ٱلنَّهَارِ (at the two ends of the day) وَزُلَفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ (and in the portions of the night near its beginning and end).

إِنَّ ٱلْحَسَنَـٰتِ يُذْهِبْنَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ (Good deeds remove bad deeds). Which good deeds? The context tells us—even though this is a separate sentence, Allah ﷻ in His wisdom didn't make it a separate ayah. Why? Because we're being told: you want to get rid of bad deeds? Commit to salah.

For the Sahaba (RA), their understanding of salah was something else entirely. They saw salah as what would give them strength to carry on with their mission. They weren't looking for strength anywhere else—only in connection with Allah ﷻ. ذَٰلِكَ ذِكْرَىٰ لِلذَّٰكِرِينَ (That is the ultimate reminder for those who actively remember).

translation: And be patient, for indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good
commentary:
وَٱصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

Remain patient, for indeed Allah ﷻ does not waste the compensation of those who excel. These believers who were excelling in their mission saw no results in Makkah—no conversions, no political change, only persecution. Yet Allah ﷻ says: not a single second of effort you've made has gone unnoticed.

We saw detailed accounts of this in Surah Tawbah: not a step you take, not a valley you cross that Allah ﷻ doesn't know about and hasn't recorded. Now a summary of that principle is given: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ.

translation: So why were there not among the generations before you those of enduring discrimination forbidding corruption on earth - except a few of those We saved from among them? But those who wronged pursued what luxury they were given therein, and they were criminals
commentary:
فَلَوْلَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ أُو۟لُوا۟ بَقِيَّةٍ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْفَسَادِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّنْ أَنجَيْنَا مِنْهُمْ ۗ وَٱتَّبَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مَآ أُتْرِفُوا۟ فِيهِ وَكَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ

How come it wasn't the case that from the generations before you, there were people of permanence, people of consistency—أُو۟لُوا۟ بَقِيَّةٍ—who would forbid corruption in the land? إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّنْ أَنجَيْنَا مِنْهُمْ (Except for a few that We rescued among them).

وَٱتَّبَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مَآ أُتْرِفُوا۟ فِيهِ (The wrongdoers followed what luxuries they were made to enjoy). The word مُتْرَف refers to one given extensive luxury. The financial elite of a society are called المُتْرَفُون (al-mutrafun). In Surah al-Isra', Allah ﷻ reveals a profound social and political philosophy: when He decides to destroy a town, He gives its financial elite political power. When the wealthy become politically dominant, they only protect the interests of the wealthy, creating a gap between rich and poor until that society collapses on itself.

Here, the wrongdoers—average people engaged in sin—followed those enjoying luxury. Why? Because they wanted to be like the rich and famous, to dress like them, talk like them, act like them, imitate them in every way. وَكَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (And they had been criminals).

translation: And your Lord would not have destroyed the cities unjustly while their people were reformers
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ لِيُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ بِظُلْمٍ وَأَهْلُهَا مُصْلِحُونَ

Your Master would not destroy towns unjustly while their people were reformers. Notice Allah ﷻ didn't say وَأَهْلُهَا صَالِحُونَ (while its people were righteous). He said مُصْلِحُونَ (reformers, those creating good).

If you're only good yourself, that's one thing. But if you're extending good into society, doing good to others, spreading goodness among others—then you're a muslih, a reformer. This is the quality that protects a society from destruction.

translation: And if your Lord had willed, He could have made mankind one community; but they will not cease to differ
commentary:
وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً ۖ وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ

Had your Master wanted, He would have made all people a single nation. وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ (However, they continue to differ among each other).

translation: Except whom your Lord has given mercy, and for that He created them. But the word of your Lord is to be fulfilled that, "I will surely fill Hell with jinn and men all together
commentary:
إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ رَبُّكَ ۚ وَلِذَٰلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ ۗ وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ

إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ رَبُّكَ (Except for those Your Master has shown mercy to). In other words, disagreement and division are done by those who are wrong and those within disbelief. The small group of believers, even though they're the minority, are the people who don't disagree—not with each other, and not with Allah ﷻ. They're in agreement with Allah ﷻ.

وَلِذَٰلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ (And for that He created them). The reason Allah ﷻ created humanity was so He could show them mercy. This statement is profound, and what makes it even more remarkable is what comes immediately after: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (The word of your Master has been completed: I will fill Hell with jinn and human beings altogether).

This placement is shocking. He just said He created us to show us mercy, and immediately after says He will fill Hell with jinn and humanity. How do we reconcile these two statements?

Consider this example: A teacher knows a student very well. This student doesn't do homework, comes late every day, doesn't take any tests. The teacher has worked with him for years and knows him deeply. Based on this knowledge—not wishes, but knowledge—the teacher says, "This student is going to fail this exam." When the student fails, the teacher's word came true. But did the teacher wish for the student to fail? Did the teacher want him to succeed? Is the teacher a criminal for knowing the student well? Is accurate knowledge a lack of mercy?

Allah ﷻ doesn't just know based on past experience—He knows with perfect, complete knowledge. Knowing what He knows, He knows the vast majority of people will end up in Hellfire. It's a tragedy, because right before stating this reality, He tells us: that's not why I made you. I made you so you could earn Paradise. I created you so you could be shown mercy.

translation: And each [story] We relate to you from the news of the messengers is that by which We make firm your heart. And there has come to you, in this, the truth and an instruction and a reminder for the believers
commentary:
وَكُلًّا نَّقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلرُّسُلِ مَا نُثَبِّتُ بِهِۦ فُؤَادَكَ ۚ وَجَآءَكَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَقُّ وَمَوْعِظَةٌ وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Now Allah ﷻ turns to the Messenger ﷺ with deeply powerful words: Everything We have revealed to you, everything We've narrated to you from the stories of the messengers—نُثَبِّتُ بِهِۦ فُؤَادَكَ (is there to keep your heart firm and strengthen your chest).

You could become overwhelmed and over-excited in delivering this message. You could run out of energy. Anyone who has volunteered for Islamic programs knows this reality—you get energized, you work intensely, and eventually you run out of steam. You need a break. If anyone had a right to be overburdened and burnt out, it was the Messenger ﷺ.

So Allah ﷻ gave him something to keep him going: the stories of previous prophets. These narratives keep your heart burning, keep you fired up and energized. You learn about their stories and see your own story in them, and you say, "I have to keep going." These are motivational reminders from Allah ﷻ to His Messenger ﷺ.

وَجَآءَكَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَقُّ وَمَوْعِظَةٌ وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (In this has come to you the truth, profound counsel, and a powerful reminder for those who believe).

## Ayah 121-122

وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنَّا عَـٰمِلُونَ / وَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ

Final words to those who disbelieve: Tell those who don't believe: ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ (Keep working in your position, stand your ground, do what you're going to do). We heard similar words from previous prophets. Now the people of Quraysh hear the same.

إِنَّا عَـٰمِلُونَ (We are busy with our own work). وَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ (You go ahead and wait; we are also waiting. Let's see how things turn out).

translation: And say to those who do not believe, "Work according to your position; indeed, we are working
And wait, indeed, we are waiting

And to Allah belong the unseen [aspects] of the heavens and the earth and to Him will be returned the matter, all of it, so worship Him and rely upon Him. And your Lord is not unaware of that which you do
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُ ٱلْأَمْرُ كُلُّهُۥ فَٱعْبُدْهُ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَيْهِ ۚ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

To Allah ﷻ alone belongs the unseen of the heavens and the earth. To Him alone the entire matter will be returned. Where this surah began is where it concludes. فَٱعْبُدْهُ (Then enslave yourself to Him).

Notice: Allah ﷻ commanded the disbelievers, but they didn't listen. So He turns to the Messenger ﷺ and says: Even if they're not listening, you still place your trust in Allah ﷻ. وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَيْهِ (Place your complete trust and reliance upon Him).

وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (Your Master is not unaware at all of what they are doing, of what all these people are up to). Allah ﷻ sees everything, knows everything, and His plan will unfold perfectly.
